Consent
Unlike subject matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction can be established by a party’s consent. Plaintiffs consent to personal jurisdiction by filing the lawsuit.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant may agree in advance by contract to submit to the jurisdiction of the court if a lawsuit is brought by the plaintiff. Such contractual consent is not effective if the court determines that the contract was a contract of adhesion. A defendant may also stipulate to personal jurisdiction once an action is brought. Some states require a party to consent to personal jurisdiction to register to do business in the state. In such states, registering to do business in the state constitutes express consent to any suits in that state.

Such contractual consent is not effective if the court determines that the contract was a contract of adhesion. A defendant may also stipulate to personal jurisdiction once an action is brought. Some states require a party to consent to personal jurisdiction to register to do business in the state. In such states, registering to do business in the state constitutes express consent to any suits in that state.

rules.rule_statement: Authorizing an agent to accept service of process in a state also constitutes consent to personal jurisdiction in that state. Usually, a state requires nonresidents doing business in a heavily regulated industry to appoint an agent. When an agent is appointed for process, either by contract or by statute, the defendant is not subject to personal jurisdiction if the agent does not advise the defendant of service of process. This rule does not apply when defendants select their own agents.

rules.rule_statement: Rule In personam jurisdiction is the power that a court has over an individual party. It is required when a judgment is sought that would impose an obligation on a defendant personally. A court with personam jurisdiction over a party may issue a judgment against the party personally, and the judgment can be satisfied by seizure of the party’s assets.

rules.rule_statement: If authorized by statute, a court can have jurisdiction over a person who is domiciled in the forum state (i.e., state in which the court is located) even if the person is temporarily absent from that state. This statutory authorization can be enacted retroactively to apply to a cause of action arising before the enactment of the statute. The same rules of domicile discussed in § I.C.3. Citizenship of Parties, supra, are applicable here.

Domicile
If authorized by statute, a court can have jurisdiction over a person who is domiciled in the forum state (i.e., state in which the court is located) even if the person is temporarily absent from that state. This statutory authorization can be enacted retroactively to apply to a cause of action arising before the enactment of the statute. The same rules of domicile discussed in § I.C.3. Citizenship of Parties, supra, are applicable here.

rules.rule_statement: A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most states have enacted statutes that authorize personal jurisdiction over nonresidents who engage in some activity in the state or cause some action to occur within the state. In many states, the long-arm statute either directly authorizes or has been interpreted as authorizing jurisdiction to the extent permissible under the Due Process Clause. Therefore, a federal court in those states need only determine whether the exercise of personal jurisdiction comports with due process. In some states, the long-arm statute confers jurisdiction only over specific activities undertaken in the state (e.g., owning property, committing a tort, or entering into a contract to supply goods or services in the state). In these states, before conducting a due process analysis, a federal court must first determine whether the statutory requirements have been met.
rules.short_rule_statement: A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most states have enacted statutes that authorize...
rules.source_text:
Long-Arm Statutes
A long-arm statute is a state statute that gives courts within the state personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants. Most states have enacted statutes that authorize personal jurisdiction over nonresidents who engage in some activity in the state or cause some action to occur within the state. In many states, the long-arm statute either directly authorizes or has been interpreted as authorizing jurisdiction to the extent permissible under the Due Process Clause. Therefore, a federal court in those states need only determine whether the exercise of personal jurisdiction comports with due process. In some states, the long-arm statute confers jurisdiction only over specific activities undertaken in the state (e.g., owning property, committing a tort, or entering into a contract to supply goods or services in the state). In these states, before conducting a due process analysis, a federal court must first determine whether the statutory requirements have been met.

rules.rule_statement: Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. See § II.C.2. Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.source_text:
Attachment
Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. See § II.C.2. Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction, infra.

rules.short_rule_statement: In general, due process requirements are satisfied if:

Due Process Requirements
In general, due process requirements are satisfied if:

rules.canonical_name: The Nonresident Defendant Has Sufficient Minimum Contacts With The Forum State;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The maintenance of the action does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. EXAM NOTE: When analyzing a personal jurisdiction question, focus on (1) the contacts that the defendant has or had with the forum state and (2) whether the assertion of jurisdiction by the court would comport with fair play and substantial justice.  Personal jurisdiction will depend on the facts of each case. Note that Shaffer expressly excluded actions to enforce previous judgments by such remedies as attachment, garnishment, and sequestration from the requirement of minimum contacts with the defendant.

The Nonresident Defendant Has Sufficient Minimum Contacts With The Forum State;
ii) The maintenance of the action does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. EXAM NOTE: When analyzing a personal jurisdiction question, focus on (1) the contacts that the defendant has or had with the forum state and (2) whether the assertion of jurisdiction by the court would comport with fair play and substantial justice.  Personal jurisdiction will depend on the facts of each case. rules.rule_statement: To warrant the assertion of in personam jurisdiction, a defendant’s contacts with the forum state must be purposeful and substantial, such that the defendant should reasonably anticipate (foresee) being taken to court there. Foreseeability depends on whether defendants recognize or anticipate that by running their business, they risk being a party to a suit in a particular state. This test is known as the purposeful availment requirement. Specific personal jurisdiction refers to a court’s authority over a defendant that arises from the defendant’s case-specific activities. Specific personal jurisdiction may be warranted over a cause of action that arises out of or closely relates to a defendant’s contact with the forum state. A strict causal relationship between a plaintiff’s cause of action and a defendant’s contacts with the forum state is not required, so long as “there is a strong relationship among the defendant, the forum, and the litigation.” Some courts have permitted specific personal jurisdiction over insurers when a car accident occurs within the territorial scope of the policy’s coverage. This applies even if the insurance company has no other contacts with the state. General personal jurisdiction refers to a court’s authority over a defendant even when the cause of action has no relationship with the defendant’s contacts with the forum state. General personal jurisdiction requires that a defendant:

rules.canonical_name: Be Domiciled In The Forum State;

components.content: Challenges with the purposeful availment requirement
components.source_text: 3)      Challenges with the purposeful availment requirement

rules.rule_statement: ii) Have continuous and systematic contacts with the forum state. There are situations when it is difficult to discern whether purposeful availment is present. For example, consider a product that is put into the stream of commerce by a person who purchased the product from the manufacturer and incorporated it in the person’s own product, which is then put into the stream of commerce. Did the manufacturer avail itself of the benefits and laws of the jurisdiction in which the product containing the manufacturer’s product is used? The Court has not been able to reach a cohesive answer. In Asahi, while four justices found sufficiency with the mere knowledge that the product being sold would end up in the forum state, four other justices believed that the manufacturer needed to take an additional step to have availed itself of the forum. A majority of the justices concluded that, even assuming the manufacturer’s conduct constituted purposeful availment, it violated the notions of fair play and substantial justice for the manufacturer (a foreign corporation) to be subject to the jurisdiction of the forum court. Asahi Metal Industry Co. v. Superior Court of California, 480 U.S. 102 (1987). See § b. Fair Play and Substantial Justice, below. Under some circumstances, the contacts of one defendant with the forum state may be imputed to another defendant for the purposes of determining jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Contacts by a nonresident employer’s agents or employees are generally imputed to the employer when the agent or employee is acting within the scope of the agency or employment. An out-of-state corporation is generally not subject to personal jurisdiction solely because of contacts in the state by an independent contractor.

rules.source_text:
To Employer
Contacts by a nonresident employer’s agents or employees are generally imputed to the employer when the agent or employee is acting within the scope of the agency or employment. An out-of-state corporation is generally not subject to personal jurisdiction solely because of contacts in the state by an independent contractor.

rules.rule_statement: Each partner is generally an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. Therefore, a partner’s activities on behalf of the partnership can confer personal jurisdiction over the partnership entity. However, the contacts may not necessarily establish personal jurisdiction over that partner or the other partners of the partnership in their individual capacities.

Each partner is generally an agent of the partnership for the purpose of its business. Therefore, a partner’s activities on behalf of the partnership can confer personal jurisdiction over the partnership entity. However, the contacts may not necessarily establish personal jurisdiction over that partner or the other partners of the partnership in their individual capacities.

rules.rule_statement: An out-of-state corporation’s contacts with the forum state will not automatically establish jurisdiction over a wholly owned subsidiary of the corporation, and contacts by a wholly owned subsidiary will not automatically confer jurisdiction over a corporate parent. However, if the subsidiary is the corporate parent’s alter ego or is specifically acting as the corporate parent’s agent, then its contacts may be imputed to the corporate parent.

However, if the subsidiary is the corporate parent’s alter ego or is specifically acting as the corporate parent’s agent, then its contacts may be imputed to the corporate parent.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located.” A federal court must generally determine personal jurisdiction as if it were a court of the state in which it is situated. Therefore, a federal court will look to state jurisdictional statutes to determine whether it has personal jurisdiction over the parties before it. See § II.B.2.d. Long- Arm Statutes, infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general...
rules.source_text:
General Rule
Under Rule 4(k)(1)(A), the service of a summons in a federal action establishes personal jurisdiction over a defendant “who is subject to the jurisdiction of a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located.” A federal court must generally determine personal jurisdiction as if it were a court of the state in which it is situated. Therefore, a federal court will look to state jurisdictional statutes to determine whether it has personal jurisdiction over the parties before it. See § II.B.2.d. Long- Arm Statutes, infra.

rules.rule_statement: While a federal court generally is not vested with nationwide personal jurisdiction, federal court does have national personal jurisdiction when authorized by federal statute (e.g., for federal statutory interpleader actions). Rule 4(k)(1)(C). See § VI.C.2.b. In Personam Jurisdiction, infra.

Nationwide Personal Jurisdiction

rules.rule_statement: For the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, a corporation is a resident corporation only if it is incorporated in the forum state. Any action may be brought against a corporation that is incorporated in the forum state. If the corporation is not incorporated in the state, then, for the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, it will constitute a foreign corporation.

rules.source_text:
Resident Corporations
For the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, a corporation is a resident corporation only if it is incorporated in the forum state. Any action may be brought against a corporation that is incorporated in the forum state. If the corporation is not incorporated in the state, then, for the purposes of in personam jurisdiction, it will constitute a foreign corporation.

rules.rule_statement: The rules of minimum contacts and substantial fairness apply to foreign corporations. To determine whether the corporation is subject to a state’s general jurisdiction, the proper inquiry is whether a corporation’s affiliations with the forum state are so “continuous and systematic” as to render the corporation essentially “at home” in the forum state. rules.rule_statement: It is generally accepted that merely having a website does not subject a defendant to process everywhere that the site can be viewed. Most courts base jurisdiction over a nonresident’s website on the degree of interactivity between the website and the forum state. Merely making a website accessible to the public and posting information is generally considered passive on a sliding scale and should not result in personal jurisdiction. However, websites that are integral to a defendant’s business are on the other end of the sliding scale. When a website’s purpose is to assist in conducting direct business transactions, courts are more likely to find minimum contacts with a state and assert personal jurisdiction. Example: In the Zippo case, the defendant was a nonresident corporation that operated an integral website with commercial contacts to more than 3,000 Pennsylvania residents and internet service providers. The court found a high level of interactivity with Pennsylvania and determined that there was personal jurisdiction over the defendant in Pennsylvania. The law in this area continues to evolve.

rules.canonical_name: Contract As Signiﬁcant Factor In Establishing Minimum Contacts
rules.rule_statement: The fact that one party to a contract is a resident of the forum state will not by itself confer personal jurisdiction over the nonresident party to the contract. Nonetheless, the existence of the contract can be a significant factor in determining whether minimum contacts exist and support a finding that the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the nonresident is appropriate.

Contract As Signiﬁcant Factor In Establishing Minimum Contacts
The fact that one party to a contract is a resident of the forum state will not by itself confer personal jurisdiction over the nonresident party to the contract. Nonetheless, the existence of the contract can be a significant factor in determining whether minimum contacts exist and support a finding that the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the nonresident is appropriate.

rules.rule_statement: If the contract contains a choice-of-law provision indicating that the forum state’s law is to be used in any action regarding the contract, then this will be a significant factor in finding jurisdiction. Such a choice-of-law provision establishes that the nonresident purposefully availed itself of the benefits of the forum state’s laws.

Choice-Of-Law Provision
If the contract contains a choice-of-law provision indicating that the forum state’s law is to be used in any action regarding the contract, then this will be a significant factor in finding jurisdiction. Such a choice-of-law provision establishes that the nonresident purposefully availed itself of the benefits of the forum state’s laws.

rules.canonical_name: Contracts Of Adhesion Or Procured By Fraud
rules.rule_statement: If the contract is adhesive or was procured through fraud, then personal jurisdiction based on the contract is not appropriate.

rules.rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction may also be proper when a defendant commits an intentional tort in his own state, but the conduct was aimed at the plaintiff in the forum state. When a defendant intentionally harms a forum state resident, a court may apply the “Calder Effects Test,” which allows personal jurisdiction over a party whose conduct was expressly aimed at the forum state, knowing that the brunt of the harmful effects would be felt there. Burnham v. Superior Court of California, 495 U.S. 604 (1990). McGee v. Int’l Life Ins. Co., 355 U.S. 220 (1957). International Shoe Co. v. State of Washington, 326 U.S. 310 (1945). See Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. at 210. World-Wide Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson, 444 U.S. 286 (1980). Ford Motor Co. v. Montana Eighth Jud. Dist. Ct. 529 U.S. ___, 141 S. Ct. 1017 (2021). Rossman v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 832 F.2d 282 (4th Cir. 1987); Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Portage La Prairie Mut. Ins. Co., 907 F.2d 911 (9th Cir. 1990) (litigation in a foreign forum is foreseeable under a contract to indemnify and defend the insured nationwide). Helicopteros Nacionales de Colombia, S.A. v. Hall, 466 U.S. 408 (1984). Cannon Mfg. Co. v. Cudahy Packing Co., 267 U.S. 333 (1925). Omni Capital Int’l, Ltd. v. Rudolf Wolff & Co., 484 U.S. 97 (1987). See Mississippi Publ’g Corp. v. Murphree, 326 U.S. 438 (1946); Robertson v. R.R. Labor Bd., 268 U.S. 619 (1925). Rossman v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 832 F.2d 282 (4th Cir. 1987); Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Portage La Prairie Mut. Ins. Co., 907 F.2d 911 (9th Cir. 1990) (litigation in a foreign forum is foreseeable under a contract to indemnify and defend the insured nationwide). Daimler AG v. Bauman, 571 U.S. 117 (2014) (emphasis added). Compare BNSF Ry. v. Tyrrell, 137 S. Ct. 1549 (2017) (foreign railroad company’s ownership of over 2,000 miles of track and employment of over 2,000 persons in a state not sufficient to render corporation at home when about 94% of the track it owned and more than 95% of its workforce were out-of-state) with Perkins v. Benguet Consol. Mining Co., 342 U.S. 437 (1952) (a foreign mining company that relocated its management activities (e.g., directors’ meetings, business correspondence, banking, stock transfers, payment of salaries, purchasing of machinery) during World War II to a state was at home in that state). See Zippo Mfg. Co. v. Zippo Dot Com, Inc., 952 F. Supp. 1119 (W.D. Pa. 1997). Burger King v. Rudzewicz, 471 U.S. 462 (1985). Calder v. Jones, 465 U.S. 783 (1984).

rules.rule_statement: Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.
rules.source_text:
Jurisdiction Over Things
Historically, jurisdiction over property has been divided into in rem jurisdiction and quasi-in-rem jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1) forfeiture and eminent domain actions initiated by the government, and (2) quiet-title actions.
rules.short_rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1)...
rules.source_text:
In Rem Jurisdiction
In rem jurisdiction is the authority of a court to determine issues concerning rights in property, either real or personal. Example: Examples of in-rem proceedings include (1) forfeiture and eminent domain actions initiated by the government, and (2) quiet-title actions.

rules.rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the forum state or who are unknown. Often, no parties are named, and the case is known by the name of the property at issue.
rules.short_rule_statement: In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the...
rules.source_text:
General Rule
In rem jurisdiction allows the court to make determinations concerning property rights that are conclusive against all potential claimants, even persons who are outside of the forum state or who are unknown. Often, no parties are named, and the case is known by the name of the property at issue.

rules.rule_statement: While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in the property. In general, for in rem jurisdiction to exist, the property at issue must be in the forum state. In rem jurisdiction can be challenged if the property is fraudulently brought into the state. It is not sufficient to simply post the notice on the property or publish the notice in a newspaper regarding persons who are known to have an interest in the property and whose whereabouts are reasonably ascertainable. Additionally, any absent defendant who does not personally receive notice may set aside the judgment at any time within one year of the final judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in...
rules.source_text:
Due Process
While in-rem proceedings are commenced against property, they must satisfy due process requirements for personal jurisdiction because they affect the rights of individuals in the property. In general, for in rem jurisdiction to exist, the property at issue must be in the forum state. In rem jurisdiction can be challenged if the property is fraudulently brought into the state. It is not sufficient to simply post the notice on the property or publish the notice in a newspaper regarding persons who are known to have an interest in the property and whose whereabouts are reasonably ascertainable. Additionally, any absent defendant who does not personally receive notice may set aside the judgment at any time within one year of the final judgment.

rules.rule_statement: Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a quasi-in-rem action was not personally binding on the defendant, could not be sued upon in any other court, and could not be enforced by seizing any of the defendant’s property other than the property at issue in the quasi-in-rem action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a...
rules.source_text:
Quasi-In-Rem Jurisdiction
Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines only the interests of the parties to the action regarding property located in the forum state. Traditionally, a judgment from a quasi-in-rem action was not personally binding on the defendant, could not be sued upon in any other court, and could not be enforced by seizing any of the defendant’s property other than the property at issue in the quasi-in-rem action.

rules.rule_statement: In a quasi-in-rem action, as with in rem and in personam actions, the defendant whose property is subject to the judgment generally must have sufficient minimum contacts with the forum state to justify the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the matter. When the action directly relates to property rights, such as the foreclosure of a mortgage or other lien, the minimum contacts requirement is satisfied because of the relationship between the claim and the property. Provided the owner of the property is given proper notice and an opportunity to be heard, the judgment is personally binding on the defendant. If the defendant chooses not to appear, the judgment of a federal district court is confined to the property that is the subject of the action. When the underlying action does not relate to property rights, but the property instead serves only as the relief sought, the minimum contacts requirement likely is not satisfied, and the court lacks personal jurisdiction to adjudicate the matter. An example is a breach-of-contract action in which the contract does not involve the in- state property.
rules.short_rule_statement: In a quasi-in-rem action, as with in rem and in personam actions, the defendant whose property is subject to the judgment generally must have sufficient minimum contacts with...
rules.source_text:
Due Process
In a quasi-in-rem action, as with in rem and in personam actions, the defendant whose property is subject to the judgment generally must have sufficient minimum contacts with the forum state to justify the exercise of personal jurisdiction over the matter. When the action directly relates to property rights, such as the foreclosure of a mortgage or other lien, the minimum contacts requirement is satisfied because of the relationship between the claim and the property. Provided the owner of the property is given proper notice and an opportunity to be heard, the judgment is personally binding on the defendant. If the defendant chooses not to appear, the judgment of a federal district court is confined to the property that is the subject of the action. rules.rule_statement: Attachment jurisdiction is a special type of quasi-in-rem jurisdiction. Historically, a plaintiff asserting a personal claim against a defendant could use an attachment of property owned by the defendant and located in the forum state as a device to obtain jurisdiction. But under the modern approach, there must be minimum contacts between the defendant and the forum state to establish jurisdiction. Under the modern rule, if the claim is not related to the ownership of the attached property, mere ownership of the attached property is generally not sufficient to satisfy the minimum contacts test. If there are such contacts, then the court generally has in personam jurisdiction over the defendant, and attachment jurisdiction is unnecessary. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). Walker v. City of Hutchison, 352 U.S. 112 (1956). § 1655. Shaffer v. Heitner, 433 U.S. 186 (1977). § 1655.

rules.canonical_name: Notice And Opportunity To Be Heard
rules.rule_statement: Rule Due process requires that deprivation of property by adjudication be preceded by notice and an opportunity for a hearing appropriate to the case.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Due process requires that deprivation of property by adjudication be preceded by notice and an opportunity for a hearing appropriate to the case.

rules.rule_statement: Due process is met if the notice is “reasonably calculated, under all the circumstances, to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an opportunity to present their objections.”

Due Process
Due process is met if the notice is “reasonably calculated, under all the circumstances, to apprise interested parties of the pendency of the action and afford them an opportunity to present their objections.”
topic_path: Notice And Opportunity To Be Heard

rules.rule_statement: If the identity and address of an interested party are known or obtainable through reasonable efforts, then notice through in-person delivery, registered mail, return receipt requested, or some other means likely to notify the particular individual is required. If the plaintiff knows that the defendant did not receive notice, then the plaintiff cannot proceed unless there are no other reasonable methods to notify the defendant. If the identity or address of an interested party is not obtainable through reasonable efforts, then other means may be satisfactory (e.g., publication of notice in newspapers). The general constitutional test is what is reasonable under the circumstances. The standards are less strict for in rem and quasi-in-rem cases than for in personam cases. When there are multiple defendants, each defendant must be served, but the manner of service will depend on whether their identities and addresses are known or unknown. When an agent has been appointed for process, either by contract or by statute, the defendant will not be subject to personal jurisdiction if the agent does not advise the defendant of service of process. This rule does not apply when defendants select their own agents.

rules.rule_statement: Satisfying due process is not the only standard for the sufficiency of notice. Both state and federal courts have procedural rules that dictate the form and service of process. While a particular form of notice may meet due process standards, it must also meet the specific procedural requirements that govern in the court where the action will be heard. For example, certified mail meets due process standards, but not every court system permits it to be used.

For example, certified mail meets due process standards, but not every court system permits it to be used.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to requiring notice of the claim, due process requires that a defendant be given an opportunity to be heard when there is a state-sponsored interference with a defendant’s property interest. The state must actively participate in the interference for due process to apply. Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co., 339 U.S. 306 (1950). Mullane v. Central Hanover Bank & Trust Co., 339 U.S. 306 (1950). Mennonite Bd. of Missions v. Adams, 462 U.S. 791 (1983). Jones v. Flowers, 547 U.S. 220 (2006).

rules.rule_statement: A general appearance occurs when a defendant in some manner recognizes the authority of the court to litigate the merits of the case. A special appearance is a procedure by which a defendant appears before a court to challenge personal jurisdiction rather than to litigate the merits of the case. General appearance operates as a consent to personal jurisdiction. However, a defendant making a special appearance is not considered to have consented to jurisdiction as long as the defendant contests personal jurisdiction in the initial pleadings. Although many states still permit special appearances, under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, special appearances have been abolished. Instead, the Rules allow a party entering a general appearance to make a motion to dismiss the claim for lack of personal jurisdiction under Rule 12(b)(2) without becoming subject to jurisdiction on that basis.
rules.short_rule_statement: A general appearance occurs when a defendant in some manner recognizes the authority of the court to litigate the merits of the case. A special appearance is a procedure by...
rules.source_text:
Special Appearance
A general appearance occurs when a defendant in some manner recognizes the authority of the court to litigate the merits of the case. A special appearance is a procedure by which a defendant appears before a court to challenge personal jurisdiction rather than to litigate the merits of the case. General appearance operates as a consent to personal jurisdiction. However, a defendant making a special appearance is not considered to have consented to jurisdiction as long as the defendant contests personal jurisdiction in the initial pleadings. Although many states still permit special appearances, under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, special appearances have been abolished. Instead, the Rules allow a party entering a general appearance to make a motion to dismiss the claim for lack of personal jurisdiction under Rule 12(b)(2) without becoming subject to jurisdiction on that basis.

rules.canonical_name: Effect Of A Denial Of Objection To Personal Jurisdiction
rules.rule_statement: If the court overrules a defendant’s objection to personal jurisdiction, most states permit the defendant to defend the case on the merits and still preserve the objection to personal jurisdiction as an issue for appeal.

For example, if a default judgment has been entered against a defendant in an action in one state, and the plaintiff sues to enforce the judgment in another state, then the defendant may collaterally attack the judgment based on lack of personal jurisdiction. However, if the defendant had both notice and an opportunity to be heard, then a collateral attack on the judgment is not permitted under the doctrine of res judicata. See § IX.D. rules.rule_statement: Most states provide immunity from service of process to nonresidents who enter the state for the purposes of attending a trial or deposition as a witness, party, or attorney. The federal courts also generally provide immunity from federal suit under the same circumstances. Baldwin v. Iowa State Traveling Men’s Ass’n, 283 U.S. 522 (1931).

rules.rule_statement: Venue refers to the proper place for an action to be brought. Venue addresses which court among the various courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction would be the proper forum for hearing a matter.

Venue
Venue refers to the proper place for an action to be brought. Venue addresses which court among the various courts having personal and subject matter jurisdiction would be the proper forum for hearing a matter.

rules.rule_statement: Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules determine the proper geographic district (e.g., Central District of California) where a trial should occur. Each state has at least one federal judicial district, and a few states have as many as four.
rules.short_rule_statement: Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules...
rules.source_text:
In General
Venue requirements are statutory and are intended to ensure the parties a fair and convenient forum for litigating their dispute. For cases in federal court, the venue rules determine the proper geographic district (e.g., Central District of California) where a trial should occur. Each state has at least one federal judicial district, and a few states have as many as four.

rules.rule_statement: The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where the property is located. Transitory actions involve a cause of action based on events that could have taken place anywhere. Many state venue statutes continue to apply this distinction. However, in federal court, venue is determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory. § 1391(a)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where...

Local And Transitory Actions
The common law created a distinction between local and transitory actions for venue. In general, local actions involve title to property and must be brought in a court where the property is located. Transitory actions involve a cause of action based on events that could have taken place anywhere. Many state venue statutes continue to apply this distinction. However, in federal court, venue is determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory. § 1391(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: A defendant that is an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued, regardless of whether incorporated, is deemed to reside in any judicial district where the entity is subject to personal jurisdiction regarding the civil action in question. § 1391(c)(2). In a state that contains multiple judicial districts and in which a defendant corporation is subject to personal jurisdiction when the action is commenced, the corporation “shall be deemed to reside in any district in that State within which its contacts would be sufficient to subject it to personal jurisdiction if that district were a separate State.” If there is no such district, then the corporation is deemed to reside in the district with which it has the most significant contacts. § 1391(d). Example: A corporation is incorporated in Delaware and has its principal place of business in Colorado. The corporation maintains a regional sales office in Orlando, Florida (U.S. Middle District Court in Florida) but does not conduct activities anywhere else in Florida. The corporation is sued for breach of a contract executed in the Orlando office and performed in Orlando. For federal venue purposes, the corporation resides in the District of Delaware, the District of Colorado, and the Middle District of Florida. Other than the Middle District, the corporation is not a resident of any other judicial district in Florida. If the entity is a plaintiff, then it is deemed to reside only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business. § 1391(c)(2). EXAM NOTE: Keep in mind that, regarding partnerships and unincorporated associations, the “entity” approach is taken only for venue.  It does not apply for determining diversity jurisdiction, when a partnership or unincorporated association is considered a citizen of each state in which each of its partners or members is domiciled.

rules.canonical_name: Change Of Venue When The Original Venue Is Proper
rules.rule_statement: Change of venue when the original venue is proper may be ordered upon motion of a party, including the plaintiff, or by the court on its own initiative. The right to transfer is available only when the jurisdiction and venue of the court considering the issue are proper. The burden of proof regarding the motion rests on the party seeking the transfer. § 1404(a). There is no specific time limit imposed on filing a change of venue motion. However, a court can deny a motion when a delay in filing the motion would prejudice an opposing party or was a dilatory tactic. EXAM NOTE: Section 1404(a) is essentially a codiﬁcation of the common-law doctrine of forum non conveniens for a subset of cases, allowing transfer from one proper federal forum to a more appropriate federal forum instead of requiring dismissal. When transfer from an otherwise proper venue is sought based on a forum-selection clause in a contract, the clause is accorded respect. Even though the clause itself does not render other forums legally improper, most circuit courts treat forum-selection clauses as prima facie valid, to be set aside only upon a strong showing that:

The Supreme Court has held that a forum-selection clause should be given “controlling weight in all but the most exceptional cases.” However, a few circuit courts continue to treat the clause as a significant, but not determinative, factor. EXAM NOTE:  Remember that the enforcement of a forum-selection clause may require a different remedy depending on the forum speciﬁed by the clause.  If the clause speciﬁes a federal forum, it may be enforced by a motion to transfer under §1404(a).  If the clause speciﬁes arbitration or a state or foreign forum, then §1404(a) will not apply, and the common-law doctrine of forum non conveniens may instead require the action to be dismissed.

rules.canonical_name: Change Of Venue When The Original Venue Is Improper
rules.rule_statement: If no timely objection is made to improper venue, then nothing prevents the district court from maintaining jurisdiction over the case. § 1406. Even if the court lacks personal jurisdiction over the defendant, the court is not barred from transferring a case to a different venue.
rules.short_rule_statement: If no timely objection is made to improper venue, then nothing prevents the district court from maintaining jurisdiction over the case. § 1406. Even if the court lacks personal...
rules.source_text:
Change Of Venue When The Original Venue Is Improper
If no timely objection is made to improper venue, then nothing prevents the district court from maintaining jurisdiction over the case. § 1406. Even if the court lacks personal jurisdiction over the defendant, the court is not barred from transferring a case to a different venue.

rules.canonical_name: Transfer To Another Division In The Same District

Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972). Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. United States District Court, 571 U.S. 49 (2013). Stewart Organization Inc. v. Ricoh Corp., 487 U.S. 22 (1988). rules.rule_statement: Rule The doctrine of forum non conveniens is a common-law rule that traditionally allows a court to dismiss an action, even when personal jurisdiction and venue are otherwise proper, if the court finds that: The forum would be too inconvenient for the parties and witnesses, and Another more convenient venue is available.

rules.source_text:
Forum Non Conveniens
Rule The doctrine of forum non conveniens is a common-law rule that traditionally allows a court to dismiss an action, even when personal jurisdiction and venue are otherwise proper, if the court finds that: The forum would be too inconvenient for the parties and witnesses, and Another more convenient venue is available.

rules.rule_statement: The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to outright dismissal. See § III.E.1. Change of Venue When the Original Venue Is Proper, supra. Consequently, as a common-law rule, the doctrine is now used by a federal district court primarily when the forum that is deemed most appropriate for the action is a state or foreign court, because the federal statute does not apply in that circumstance.
rules.short_rule_statement: The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to...
rules.source_text:
In General
The doctrine of forum non conveniens has been codified and relaxed regarding transfers between federal district courts, now allowing the remedy of transfer as opposed to outright dismissal. See § III.E.1. Change of Venue When the Original Venue Is Proper, supra. Consequently, as a common-law rule, the doctrine is now used by a federal district court primarily when the forum that is deemed most appropriate for the action is a state or foreign court, because the federal statute does not apply in that circumstance.

rules.rule_statement: To convince a court to invoke the doctrine of forum non conveniens, the defendant ordinarily bears a heavy burden to displace the plaintiff’s chosen forum. Further, a forum-selection clause designating a state or foreign forum modifies the conventional rule for forum non conveniens by shifting the burden to the plaintiff to show why the court should disregard the forum-selection clause. This deference for the plaintiff’s chosen forum does not apply when the plaintiff is a foreign citizen. Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. United States District Court, 571 U.S. 49 (2013). Piper Aircraft Co. v. Reyno, 454 U.S. 235 (1981).

Burden Of Proof
To convince a court to invoke the doctrine of forum non conveniens, the defendant ordinarily bears a heavy burden to displace the plaintiff’s chosen forum. Further, a forum-selection clause designating a state or foreign forum modifies the conventional rule for forum non conveniens by shifting the burden to the plaintiff to show why the court should disregard the forum-selection clause. This deference for the plaintiff’s chosen forum does not apply when the plaintiff is a foreign citizen. Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. United States District Court, 571 U.S. 49 (2013). Piper Aircraft Co. v. Reyno, 454 U.S. 235 (1981).

Substantive Law
In a diversity action, the district court is required to apply state substantive law. To determine which state’s substantive law applies, the district court must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the state in which it is located (i.e., the forum state).

rules.rule_statement: Concerning procedure in a diversity action, when a procedural issue is addressed by a valid federal law (e.g., a statute or Federal Rule of Evidence), then federal law is applied, even if a state rule or statute is in conflict. If no federal law applies, then the general rule is that the district court must follow state law regarding substance. Under certain circumstances, the district court can choose to ignore state law concerning procedure.

rules.rule_statement: When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal statutes, constitutional provisions, and federal common law as a source of the law. Federal common law is created when no applicable federal statute or constitutional provision exists. Therefore, the court must resort to source materials of the common law. No general federal common law exists, and the general areas to which federal common law is applicable will be limited.
rules.short_rule_statement: When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal...
rules.source_text:
Federal Common Law
When it is appropriate under the Erie doctrine to apply federal law (e.g., a federal question claim or a procedural issue in diversity case), the court may look to federal statutes, constitutional provisions, and federal common law as a source of the law. Federal common law is created when no applicable federal statute or constitutional provision exists. Therefore, the court must resort to source materials of the common law. No general federal common law exists, and the general areas to which federal common law is applicable will be limited.

rules.rule_statement: Federal common law may also apply in state court. If state jurisdiction is concurrent with federal question jurisdiction and federal common law would have applied in federal court, then it will also apply in state court. See § I.B.2. Concurrent Versus Exclusive Jurisdiction, supra. See, e.g., Boyle v. United Technologies Corp., 487 U.S. 500 (1988) (a federal defense contractor could assert a defense recognized under federal common law to a state products liability action brought under diversity jurisdiction because the unique interests of the U.S. government in contracting for the procurement of military equipment were directly affected and a significant conflict existed with state law); Clearfield Trust Co. v. United States, 318 U.S. 363 was later stolen and cashed because it was important to have a uniform standard for determining liability on such commercial paper). See Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001).

State-Court Cases
Federal common law may also apply in state court. If state jurisdiction is concurrent with federal question jurisdiction and federal common law would have applied in federal court, then it will also apply in state court. See § I.B.2. Concurrent Versus Exclusive Jurisdiction, supra. See, e.g., Boyle v. United Technologies Corp., 487 U.S. 500 (1988) (a federal defense contractor could assert a defense recognized under federal common law to a state products liability action brought under diversity jurisdiction because the unique interests of the U.S. government in contracting for the procurement of military equipment were directly affected and a significant conflict existed with state law); Clearfield Trust Co. v. United States, 318 U.S. 363 was later stolen and cashed because it was important to have a uniform standard for determining liability on such commercial paper). See Semtek Int’l Inc. v. Lockheed Martin Corp., 531 U.S. 497 (2001).

rules.rule_statement: After determining that state substantive law should apply, a U.S. district court with diversity jurisdiction generally must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the forum state to determine which state’s substantive law applies. Example: A U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity must apply Delaware’s conflict-of-law rules to determine which state’s substantive law applies to the action before it. However, if a district court transfers a diversity case, then the district court to which the case is transferred must apply the law that would have been applied in the district court that transferred the case. Example: A claim is initially brought in a U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity. Although venue was proper, the claim is transferred to a U.S. District Court located in California for the convenience of the parties. The U.S. District Court located in California still must apply Delaware’s conflict-of-law rules to determine which state’s substantive law applies to the action before it.

Determining Applicable State Law
After determining that state substantive law should apply, a U.S. district court with diversity jurisdiction generally must apply the conflict-of-law rules of the forum state to determine which state’s substantive law applies. Example: A U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity must apply Delaware’s conflict-of-law rules to determine which state’s substantive law applies to the action before it. However, if a district court transfers a diversity case, then the district court to which the case is transferred must apply the law that would have been applied in the district court that transferred the case. Example: A claim is initially brought in a U.S. District Court located in Delaware that is sitting in diversity. Although venue was proper, the claim is transferred to a U.S. District Court located in California for the convenience of the parties. rules.canonical_name: Highest State Court’S Rulings On Substantive Law Control
rules.rule_statement: In determining a state’s substantive law, the U.S. district court will be bound by the rulings of the state’s highest court.
rules.short_rule_statement: In determining a state’s substantive law, the U.S. district court will be bound by the rulings of the state’s highest court.
rules.source_text:
Highest State Court’S Rulings On Substantive Law Control
In determining a state’s substantive law, the U.S. district court will be bound by the rulings of the state’s highest court.
topic_path: Highest State Court’S Rulings On Substantive Law Control

rules.canonical_name: Highest State Court Not Yet Ruled
rules.rule_statement: If the state’s highest court has not spoken on an issue, the federal court must try to determine how the state’s highest court would rule on the issue if it were to consider it. To make this determination, a federal court will generally look to any lower state-court decisions that have considered the issue. The federal court will follow a lower court’s view unless it believes that the highest state court would not follow it. If no state court has considered the issue, then the federal court will have to determine how it believes the highest court in the state would rule if it looked at the issue today. Some states have procedures that allow the federal district court to certify a question of substantive law to the state supreme court for clarification.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the state’s highest court has not spoken on an issue, the federal court must try to determine how the state’s highest court would rule on the issue if it were to consider...
rules.source_text:
Highest State Court Not Yet Ruled
If the state’s highest court has not spoken on an issue, the federal court must try to determine how the state’s highest court would rule on the issue if it were to consider it. To make this determination, a federal court will generally look to any lower state-court decisions that have considered the issue. The federal court will follow a lower court’s view unless it believes that the highest state court would not follow it. If no state court has considered the issue, then the federal court will have to determine how it believes the highest court in the state would rule if it looked at the issue today. Some states have procedures that allow the federal district court to certify a question of substantive law to the state supreme court for clarification.
topic_path: Highest State Court Not Yet Ruled

rules.canonical_name: Highest Court Rules After The Federal Suit Is Complete
rules.rule_statement: After the U.S. district court action has been completed, if the state’s highest court rules on an issue in a way that is different from the way the district court predicted, then a federal appeals court is bound by the state court’s ruling.

rules.rule_statement: In diversity actions, a U.S. district court is bound by the conflict-of-laws rules of the state where the district court is located, but only to the extent that the state’s rules are valid under the Full Faith and Credit and Due Process Clauses of the U.S. Constitution. State conflict-of-laws rules frequently determine whether to apply the law of the forum state or the law of a foreign jurisdiction by considering whether the law to be applied is substantive or procedural. States apply their own procedural laws and sometimes apply the substantive law of a foreign jurisdiction. EXAM NOTE: Remember that although the substance-procedure distinction also arises in federal-state conﬂicts under Erie, it is not the same substance-procedure distinction that arises in state-state conﬂicts under Klaxon.  Some issues (e.g., burden of proof) are treated differently depending on whether they arise in a federal-state or state-state conﬂict. Questions about the following issues are generally considered procedural and controlled by the law of the forum state:
rules.short_rule_statement: In diversity actions, a U.S. district court is bound by the conflict-of-laws rules of the state where the district court is located, but only to the extent that the state’s...
rules.source_text:
Conﬂict Of Laws
In diversity actions, a U.S. district court is bound by the conflict-of-laws rules of the state where the district court is located, but only to the extent that the state’s rules are valid under the Full Faith and Credit and Due Process Clauses of the U.S. Constitution. State conflict-of-laws rules frequently determine whether to apply the law of the forum state or the law of a foreign jurisdiction by considering whether the law to be applied is substantive or procedural. States apply their own procedural laws and sometimes apply the substantive law of a foreign jurisdiction. EXAM NOTE: Remember that although the substance-procedure distinction also arises in federal-state conﬂicts under Erie, it is not the same substance-procedure distinction that arises in state-state conﬂicts under Klaxon.  Some issues (e.g., burden of proof) are treated differently depending on whether they arise in a federal-state or state-state conﬂict. rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 3, a civil action is commenced by filing a complaint with the court clerk. In a federal diversity action, state law will apply to decide when the action commenced for the purposes of the statute of limitations. Therefore, if state law provides that an action is commenced by service of process on a defendant, then the state rule will control for the purposes of diversity jurisdiction.

Manner
Under Rule 3, a civil action is commenced by filing a complaint with the court clerk. In a federal diversity action, state law will apply to decide when the action commenced for the purposes of the statute of limitations. Therefore, if state law provides that an action is commenced by service of process on a defendant, then the state rule will control for the purposes of diversity jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 6 provides guidelines for computing time limits that apply throughout the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, unless otherwise provided.

Time Computation
Rule 6 provides guidelines for computing time limits that apply throughout the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, unless otherwise provided.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 6(a)(1), when a time period is stated in days, the period excludes the day of the event that triggers the period, but it includes every day following, including intervening Saturdays, Sundays, and legal holidays. If the last day of the period is a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, then the period is extended to include the next nonweekend or nonholiday day. Rule 6(a)(1).

General Rule

If the last day of the period is a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, then the period is extended to include the next nonweekend or nonholiday day. Rule 6(a)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Service of process refers to the delivery of the summons and complaint upon the defendant. Rule 4 sets forth the procedure for service of process in a federal action, detailing the form, content, method of issuance, methods of service, and time of service of a summons and complaint. In the absence of service of process (or waiver of service by the defendant), a court ordinarily may not exercise power over a party named as a defendant in a complaint. Methods of service on individuals Serve on the defendant personally Deliver to the defendant’s usual place of abode with a person of Inside the U.S. suitable age and discretion residing there or Deliver to an authorized agent In any internationally agreed upon manner reasonably calculated to give notice, or: Any method permitted by the foreign country; Personal service on the individual, unless the foreign country’s law prohibits it: Outside the U.S. Any form of mail that (1) the clerk addresses and sends to the individual and (2) requires a signed receipt, unless prohibited by foreign country; or Any other means not prohibited by international agreement, as ordered by the court Methods of service on business organizations Inside the U.S. Delivering the summons to: An officer A managing agent or general agent An agent appointed or authorized by law to receive process Any method available for service on an individual outside the U.S., except Outside the U.S. personal delivery Methods of service on government agents and entities Deliver to the U.S. Attorney (or a designee) for the district where the action is brought, or send a copy by registered or certified mail to the civil-process clerk at the U.S. Attorney’s Office U.S. government Send a copy by registered or certified mail to (1) the U.S. Attorney General at Washington, D.C. and (2) the agency or officer, if the action challenges an order of a nonparty agency or officer of the United States Serve the United States and Federal agency or officer or employee sued in Deliver by registered or certified mail to the federal agency, official capacity officer, or employee Serve the U.S. government and Officer or employee sued individually Serve the officer or employee as an individual Deliver to the chief executive officer or State or local government Follow the state’s law for serving a summons or like process on a defendant Follow any arrangement for service between the plaintiff and foreign government or Foreign government If no arrangement exists, deliver by an applicable international convention on the service of judicial documents Exam Note In an action brought in a court of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located or where service is made, service may be made on any individual or business organization within the United States by following the state’s law for serving a summons in an action. Rule 4(h)(1)(A). If a Civil Rules emergency is declared pursuant to Rule 87 (e.g., public health emergency), a federal court may authorize service of process on (1) an individual located in the United States, (2) a corporation or association located in the United States, (3) the United States government, its agencies, corporations, officers, or employees; a state or local government, or (4) a minor or incompetent person located in the United States by a method that is reasonably calculated to give notice. Rule 87(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d), a competent individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons by waiving service. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of the summons. Waiver of service may not be requested if the defendant is a government entity, a minor, or incompetent.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d), a competent individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons by waiving...
rules.source_text:
Waiver Of Service
Under Rule 4(d), a competent individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons by waiving service. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of the summons. Waiver of service may not be requested if the defendant is a government entity, a minor, or incompetent.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process. The notice must be accompanied by a copy of the complaint, two copies of a waiver form, and a prepaid means for returning the form. Additionally, the notice must give the defendant a reasonable time of at least 30 days after the request was sent (or at least 60 days if sent to a foreign defendant) to return the waiver. Rule 4(d)(1).
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or...
rules.source_text:
Request For Waiver
A plaintiff’s notice and request for waiver of service must be in writing and be addressed to the individual defendant, or, for a corporation, to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process. The notice must be accompanied by a copy of the complaint, two copies of a waiver form, and a prepaid means for returning the form. Additionally, the notice must give the defendant a reasonable time of at least 30 days after the request was sent (or at least 60 days if sent to a foreign defendant) to return the waiver. Rule 4(d)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 4(d)(3), if a defendant timely returns a waiver of service before being served with process, then the defendant’s deadline to serve an answer to the complaint extends from 21 days to 60 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant is outside the United States and waives service, the time period for serving an answer is extended to 90 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant agrees to waive service, then the date on which the plaintiff files the waiver form with the court is deemed the date of service. Rule 4(d)(4).

The Effect Of A Waiver
Under Rule 4(d)(3), if a defendant timely returns a waiver of service before being served with process, then the defendant’s deadline to serve an answer to the complaint extends from 21 days to 60 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant is outside the United States and waives service, the time period for serving an answer is extended to 90 days after the notice was sent. If the defendant agrees to waive service, then the date on which the plaintiff files the waiver form with the court is deemed the date of service. Rule 4(d)(4).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 12(b), the defenses of insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process must be asserted in a responsive pleading or by motion before a responsive pleading is submitted. A party’s failure to object waives these objections. Rule 12(h). Rule 4(d)(5) specifically provides that waiver of service does not waive any objection to personal jurisdiction or to venue. Murphy Bros., Inc. v. Michetti Pipe Stringing, Inc., 526 U.S. 344 (1999).

rules.rule_statement: Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs the issuance of an injunction. Under Rule 65(d), every order granting injunctive relief must: State the reasons for its issuance Specifically state its terms and Specify the acts that the defendant is restrained from performing or required to perform. Additionally, to protect the adverse party against harm from an erroneous grant of preliminary relief, the Rule expresses a preference for the moving party to post an injunction bond. Rule 65(c). An injunction is considered an extraordinary remedy, only to be granted in limited circumstances.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs...
rules.source_text:
Injunctions
Rule An injunction is a form of equitable relief mandating that a defendant perform a specified act or prohibiting a defendant from performing a specified act. Rule 65 governs the issuance of an injunction. Under Rule 65(d), every order granting injunctive relief must: State the reasons for its issuance Specifically state its terms and Specify the acts that the defendant is restrained from performing or required to perform. Additionally, to protect the adverse party against harm from an erroneous grant of preliminary relief, the Rule expresses a preference for the moving party to post an injunction bond. Rule 65(c). An injunction is considered an extraordinary remedy, only to be granted in limited circumstances.

rules.rule_statement: There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory injunction, which is a specific relief, requires a person to engage in an affirmative act that typically changes the status quo. When the injunction is directed at providing mandatory relief, as opposed to preserving the status quo, the moving party’s burden is particularly heavy. A prohibitory injunction, which is a form of preventive relief, restrains or prohibits a party from engaging in a specified behavior.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory...
rules.source_text:
Types Of Injunctions
There are two types of injunctions: mandatory and prohibitory. The determining factor is the type of conduct that is affected by the injunction, if granted. A mandatory injunction, which is a specific relief, requires a person to engage in an affirmative act that typically changes the status quo. When the injunction is directed at providing mandatory relief, as opposed to preserving the status quo, the moving party’s burden is particularly heavy. A prohibitory injunction, which is a form of preventive relief, restrains or prohibits a party from engaging in a specified behavior.

rules.rule_statement: A temporary restraining order (TRO) preserves the status quo of the parties until there is an opportunity to hold a full hearing on the application for a preliminary injunction. This interlocutory injunction may remain in effect only a limited number of days, to be set by the court, and no longer than 14 days unless good cause exists or the adversary consents. Rule 65(b)(2). TROs are not generally immediately appealable, unless they have the effect of an injunction, as when the court extends the length of the TRO beyond the time limit specified in the Rules.

rules.source_text:
Temporary Restraining Order
A temporary restraining order (TRO) preserves the status quo of the parties until there is an opportunity to hold a full hearing on the application for a preliminary injunction. This interlocutory injunction may remain in effect only a limited number of days, to be set by the court, and no longer than 14 days unless good cause exists or the adversary consents. Rule 65(b)(2). TROs are not generally immediately appealable, unless they have the effect of an injunction, as when the court extends the length of the TRO beyond the time limit specified in the Rules.

rules.rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is a form of relief issued before a full hearing on the merits, but only upon notice to the defendant and a hearing on whether the injunction should issue. Should an expedited decision on the merits be appropriate, a court may order a trial on the merits to be consolidated with the preliminary injunction hearing. Rule 65(a). A plaintiff seeking a preliminary injunction must establish that:
rules.short_rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is a form of relief issued before a full hearing on the merits, but only upon notice to the defendant and a hearing on whether the injunction should...
rules.source_text:
Preliminary Injunction
A preliminary injunction is a form of relief issued before a full hearing on the merits, but only upon notice to the defendant and a hearing on whether the injunction should issue. Should an expedited decision on the merits be appropriate, a court may order a trial on the merits to be consolidated with the preliminary injunction hearing. Rule 65(a). A plaintiff seeking a preliminary injunction must establish that:

rules.rule_statement: ii) The plaintiff is likely to suffer irreparable harm in the absence of relief; iii) The balance of equities is in the plaintiff’s favor; and iv) The injunction is in the best interests of the public.

rules.rule_statement: A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person may move for modification or dissolution. The standard for a permanent injunction is essentially the same as for a preliminary injunction except that the plaintiff must show actual success on the merits.
rules.short_rule_statement: A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person...
rules.source_text:
Permanent Injunction
A permanent injunction is a judicial determination on the merits of an application for relief. Once issued, it continues until dissolved by the court, but any affected person may move for modification or dissolution. The standard for a permanent injunction is essentially the same as for a preliminary injunction except that the plaintiff must show actual success on the merits.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The complaint is the initial pleading in an action filed by the plaintiff and serves as notice to the opposing party. Under Rule 8(a), a complaint (or any pleading in which a claim is made) is generally required to include: A short and plain statement of the grounds that establish the court’s subject matter jurisdiction; A short and plain statement of the claim establishing entitlement to relief; and A demand for judgment for the relief sought by the pleader.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule The complaint is the initial pleading in an action filed by the plaintiff and serves as notice to the opposing party. Under Rule 8(a), a complaint (or any pleading in...
rules.source_text:
Complaint
Rule The complaint is the initial pleading in an action filed by the plaintiff and serves as notice to the opposing party. Under Rule 8(a), a complaint (or any pleading in which a claim is made) is generally required to include: A short and plain statement of the grounds that establish the court’s subject matter jurisdiction; A short and plain statement of the claim establishing entitlement to relief; and A demand for judgment for the relief sought by the pleader.

rules.rule_statement: A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new jurisdictional support.
rules.short_rule_statement: A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A complaint in federal court must contain an allegation of the subject matter jurisdiction of the court unless the court already has jurisdiction and the claim needs no new jurisdictional support.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 8(a)(2) generally requires only “a short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief” to “give the defendant fair notice of what [the] plaintiff’s claim is and the grounds upon which it rests.” Detailed factual allegations are not required, but a party must plead facts sufficient to show that the claim has substantive plausibility. A claimant may not merely recite the elements of a cause of action with broad, conclusory statements. A complaint should not be dismissed for an imperfect statement of the legal theory. See § V.E.2. Motion to Dismiss for Failure to State a Claim Upon Which Relief Can Be Granted, infra. Rule 9 provides certain special rules regarding pleading, which apply not just in the context of a complaint, but to any pleading. In certain circumstances (described as follows), a party is required to plead with greater detail than under the general rules. Claim Special pleading requirement Need not allege a party’s capacity to sue or be sued unless required to establish jurisdiction Capacity Must challenge capacity by specific denial stating any supporting facts that are peculiarly within the party’s knowledge Malice, intent, knowledge, or other mental condition may be alleged generally Fraud, mistake, mental condition Circumstances giving rise to fraud/mistake must be stated with particularity Claim Special pleading requirement Assertion that conditions precedent occurred may be alleged generally Conditions precedent Denial that conditions precedent occurred must be stated with particularity Sufficient to allege that the document was legally Official document or act issued or the act was legally done Judgment Sufficient to plead the judgment without showing any jurisdiction to render it General damages may be alleged generally Damages Special damages must be specifically stated Time and place Relevant and material facts regarding time and place must be specified in detail Federal courts have no power to require more specific pleading beyond following the rules provided in the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or by statute.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s complaint may include relief in the alternative and seek different types of relief (e.g., monetary damages, equitable relief, or a declaratory judgment). As a general matter, a demand for judgment in a contested case does not limit the nature or scope of relief that the trial court may grant. The plaintiff is entitled to whatever relief is appropriate for the claims alleged in the complaint and proved at trial. The exception to this general rule concerns default judgments, as a default judgment cannot “differ in kind from, or exceed in amount, what is demanded in the pleadings.” Rule 54(c). Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41, 47 (1957). See Swierkiewicz v. Sorema N.A., 534 U.S. 506 (2002).
