rules.source_text:
Demand For Relief
A plaintiff’s complaint may include relief in the alternative and seek different types of relief (e.g., monetary damages, equitable relief, or a declaratory judgment). As a general matter, a demand for judgment in a contested case does not limit the nature or scope of relief that the trial court may grant. The plaintiff is entitled to whatever relief is appropriate for the claims alleged in the complaint and proved at trial. The exception to this general rule concerns default judgments, as a default judgment cannot “differ in kind from, or exceed in amount, what is demanded in the pleadings.” Rule 54(c). Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41, 47 (1957). See Swierkiewicz v. Sorema N.A., 534 U.S. 506 (2002).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Under Rule 12, within 21 days of service of process, a defendant must respond to a complaint either by an answer or by a pre-answer motion, or the defendant must seek additional time to answer. The defendant risks a default for failing to take one of these steps.

Motions Against The Complaint
Rule Under Rule 12, within 21 days of service of process, a defendant must respond to a complaint either by an answer or by a pre-answer motion, or the defendant must seek additional time to answer. The defendant risks a default for failing to take one of these steps.

rules.source_text:
Basis
Before filing an answer, a defendant may file a motion under Rule 12(b), raising any or all of the following defenses:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Lack of personal jurisdiction; iii) Improper venue; iv) Insufficient process;
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Lack of personal jurisdiction; iii) Improper venue; iv) Insufficient process;

Lack Of Subject Matter Jurisdiction
ii) Lack of personal jurisdiction; iii) Improper venue; iv) Insufficient process;

rules.rule_statement: vi) Failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted; and vii) Failure to join a necessary or indispensable party under Rule 19. Such motions generally seek dismissal of the claim. For the defenses of insufficient process and service of process, it is common to make a motion to quash the service of process or the process itself. A motion to quash is a request that a court nullify or void a legal action, e.g., find that service of process was invalid or a subpoena was improperly issued.

Insufficient Service Of Process

For the defenses of insufficient process and service of process, it is common to make a motion to quash the service of process or the process itself. A motion to quash is a request that a court nullify or void a legal action, e.g., find that service of process was invalid or a subpoena was improperly issued.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 12 defense Timing Lack of subject matter jurisdiction May be raised at any time Lack of personal jurisdiction Must be raised: Improper venue In a pre-answer motion or Insufficient service or process If no pre-answer motion is made, within the time for amending the answer as of right Failure to state a claim upon which May be raised: relief can be granted In any pleading Failure to join a necessary or In a motion for judgment on the indispensable party under Rule 19 pleading; or At trial Except for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, the consequence of not asserting these defenses in time provided is that the defense will be waived. Rule 12(h). Although Rule 12(b) states that a motion asserting one of these defenses must be made before pleading if a responsive pleading is allowed, courts have concluded, based on the waiver provision in Rule 12(h)(1), that a motion to dismiss based on one of these defenses may be filed after an answer is filed if the answer pleads one of these defenses. Under the omnibus motion rule, when a party makes a pre-answer motion raising one of these defenses but omitting the others, the party may not make another pre- answer motion raising one of the omitted defenses that was available to the party when the earlier motion was filed. The party is deemed to have waived the excluded defenses. Rule 12(g)(2), (h)(1)(A).

rules.canonical_name: Treat All Well-Pleaded Facts Of The Complaint As True
rules.rule_statement: ii) Resolve all doubts and inferences in the plaintiff’s favor; and iii) View the pleading in the light most favorable to the plaintiff.

rules.rule_statement: The facts alleged in the complaint must “raise a right to relief above the speculative level... on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if doubtful in fact).” The complaint must state enough facts to raise a reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.
rules.short_rule_statement: The facts alleged in the complaint must “raise a right to relief above the speculative level... on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if...
rules.source_text:
More Than Speculation
The facts alleged in the complaint must “raise a right to relief above the speculative level... on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if doubtful in fact).” The complaint must state enough facts to raise a reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.

rules.rule_statement: In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:
rules.short_rule_statement: In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:
rules.source_text:
What The Court May Consider
In ruling on a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), the court may consider only:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Any exhibits attached to the complaint; and iii) Any matters subject to judicial notice. If additional evidence presented to the court (e.g., affidavits) is not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment. In a summary judgment proceeding, all parties must be given an opportunity to present all material information for the court’s consideration. Rule 12(d). Note that a court also can consider other matters regarding a motion to dismiss under other Rule 12(b) defenses.
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Any exhibits attached to the complaint; and iii) Any matters subject to judicial notice. If additional evidence presented to the court (e.g., affidavits) is not excluded...
rules.source_text:
The Allegations In The Complaint
ii) Any exhibits attached to the complaint; and iii) Any matters subject to judicial notice. If additional evidence presented to the court (e.g., affidavits) is not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment. In a summary judgment proceeding, all parties must be given an opportunity to present all material information for the court’s consideration. Rule 12(d). Note that a court also can consider other matters regarding a motion to dismiss under other Rule 12(b) defenses.

rules.rule_statement: The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions unsupported by factual allegations. This includes mere conclusory statements and assertions devoid of facts. For example, a complaint that alleges that a defendant caused an injury, without explanation as to how it occurred, does not meet the requirements of Rule 8(a) and, as a result, cannot survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion. Second, the court should assume the truth or veracity of well-pleaded factual allegations and should include a “context specific” analysis that “draw[s] on [the Court’s] judicial experience and common sense” to determine whether the allegations “plausibly give rise to an entitlement of relief.”
rules.short_rule_statement: The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions...
rules.source_text:
Two-Step Analysis
The U.S. Supreme Court has established a two-step analysis for adjudicating a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). First, the court must identify and reject legal conclusions unsupported by factual allegations. This includes mere conclusory statements and assertions devoid of facts. For example, a complaint that alleges that a defendant caused an injury, without explanation as to how it occurred, does not meet the requirements of Rule 8(a) and, as a result, cannot survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion. Second, the court should assume the truth or veracity of well-pleaded factual allegations and should include a “context specific” analysis that “draw[s] on [the Court’s] judicial experience and common sense” to determine whether the allegations “plausibly give rise to an entitlement of relief.”

rules.rule_statement: If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered, and the plaintiff can appeal. If a defendant’s motion to dismiss is denied, then the defendant may either answer the claim or allow a default judgment to be entered and then appeal.
rules.short_rule_statement: If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered,...
rules.source_text:
Outcome
If a claim is dismissed, then the plaintiff may generally amend the pleading and continue the action. If the plaintiff does not wish to do so, then a judgment will be entered, and the plaintiff can appeal. If a defendant’s motion to dismiss is denied, then the defendant may either answer the claim or allow a default judgment to be entered and then appeal.

rules.rule_statement: After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when the material facts are not in dispute and a judgment on the merits can be achieved based on the content of the pleadings.
rules.short_rule_statement: After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when...
rules.source_text:
In General
After the pleadings are closed, a party may move for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c). A motion for judgment on the pleadings allows a court to dispose of a case when the material facts are not in dispute and a judgment on the merits can be achieved based on the content of the pleadings.

rules.rule_statement: A motion under Rule 12(c) must be made after an answer is filed.
rules.short_rule_statement: A motion under Rule 12(c) must be made after an answer is filed.
rules.source_text:
Timing
A motion under Rule 12(c) must be made after an answer is filed.

rules.rule_statement: The standard for a motion under Rule 12(c) is generally the same as that for a motion under Rule 12(b)(6). Likewise, if matters outside the pleadings are not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: The standard for a motion under Rule 12(c) is generally the same as that for a motion under Rule 12(b)(6). Likewise, if matters outside the pleadings are not excluded from the...
rules.source_text:
Standard
The standard for a motion under Rule 12(c) is generally the same as that for a motion under Rule 12(b)(6). Likewise, if matters outside the pleadings are not excluded from the court’s review, then the motion must be treated as a motion for summary judgment.

rules.rule_statement: If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement. The motion must specify the defects in the pleading as well as the details sought by the party making the motion. Rule 12(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement....
rules.source_text:
Vague And Ambiguous
If a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a party cannot reasonably draft a required responsive pleading, then the responding party may move for a more definite statement. The motion must specify the defects in the pleading as well as the details sought by the party making the motion. Rule 12(e).

rules.rule_statement: Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting such a motion is whether the pleading provides enough information for the responding party to draft a responsive pleading and commence discovery. A motion for a more definite statement may be appropriate when the pleader fails to allege facts required to be specifically pleaded, such as allegations of fraud or mistake under Rule 9(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting...
rules.source_text:
Standard
Courts are generally reluctant to grant a motion for a more definite statement because discovery is available to get more information about an issue. The standard for granting such a motion is whether the pleading provides enough information for the responding party to draft a responsive pleading and commence discovery. A motion for a more definite statement may be appropriate when the pleader fails to allege facts required to be specifically pleaded, such as allegations of fraud or mistake under Rule 9(b).

rules.rule_statement: A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court order granting this motion within 14 days of the notice of the order. Rule 12(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court...
rules.source_text:
Timing
A party must make a motion for a more definite statement before filing a responsive pleading. The court may strike a party’s pleading if the party fails to respond to a court order granting this motion within 14 days of the notice of the order. Rule 12(e).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or material be stricken. The court can take such action upon motion or upon the court’s initiative. Defenses that tend to complicate litigation significantly or that are insufficient at law may be stricken to avoid unnecessary time and money in litigating invalid and spurious issues.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or...
rules.source_text:
Rule 12(F)
Under Rule 12(f), if a pleading contains any insufficient defense or redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous material, then the court may order that such defense or material be stricken. The court can take such action upon motion or upon the court’s initiative. Defenses that tend to complicate litigation significantly or that are insufficient at law may be stricken to avoid unnecessary time and money in litigating invalid and spurious issues.

rules.rule_statement: When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party must make a motion to strike within 21 days after service of the pleading.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party...
rules.source_text:
Timing
When a responsive pleading is permitted, the responding party must move to strike before responding to such a pleading. When a responsive pleading is not permitted, the party must make a motion to strike within 21 days after service of the pleading.

rules.rule_statement: Although not specifically provided for in the Rules, courts have generally allowed a party to amend a motion to dismiss to raise an omitted ground if the party acts promptly and before the court rules on the original motion. The courts reason that “an amendment made just a few days following the motion is consistent with the spirit of Rule 12(h) and neither prejudices the plaintiff nor burdens the court.” Royal Globe Ins. Co. v. Logicon, Inc., 487 F. Supp. 1245 (N.D. Ill. 1980) (lack of personal jurisdiction defense). Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 545 (2007). See, e.g., Thompson v. Chrysler Motors Corp., 755 F.2d 1162 (5th Cir. 1985) (in determining motion to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction, court can consider not only affidavits but also evidence obtained by discovery methods, such as depositions and interrogatories). Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009). See Anchor Hocking Corp. v. Jacksonville Elec. Auth., 419 F. Supp. 992. (M.D. Fla. 1976); SEC v. Gulf & Western Indus., Inc., 502 F. Supp. 343 (D.D.C. 1980). Friedman v. World Transportation, Inc., 636 F. Supp. 685 (N.D. Ill. 1986).
rules.short_rule_statement: Although not specifically provided for in the Rules, courts have generally allowed a party to amend a motion to dismiss to raise an omitted ground if the party acts promptly...
rules.source_text:
Amending A Motion
Although not specifically provided for in the Rules, courts have generally allowed a party to amend a motion to dismiss to raise an omitted ground if the party acts promptly and before the court rules on the original motion. The courts reason that “an amendment made just a few days following the motion is consistent with the spirit of Rule 12(h) and neither prejudices the plaintiff nor burdens the court.” Royal Globe Ins. Co. v. Logicon, Inc., 487 F. Supp. 1245 (N.D. Ill. 1980) (lack of personal jurisdiction defense). Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 545 (2007). See, e.g., Thompson v. Chrysler Motors Corp., 755 F.2d 1162 (5th Cir. 1985) (in determining motion to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction, court can consider not only affidavits but also evidence obtained by discovery methods, such as depositions and interrogatories). Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009). See Anchor Hocking Corp. v. Jacksonville Elec. Auth., 419 F. Supp. 992. (M.D. Fla. 1976);

rules.rule_statement: An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.
rules.short_rule_statement: An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.
rules.source_text:
Answer
An answer is a pleading by the defendant that responds to a plaintiff’s complaint. A plaintiff would also file an answer if responding to a defendant’s counterclaim.

rules.rule_statement: The answer must admit or deny the allegations of the plaintiff’s complaint. Rule 8(b). If the defendant lacks knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief concerning the truth or falsity of an allegation, then the defendant must say so in the answer. This response has the effect of a denial under Rule 8(b). Before pleading lack of sufficient knowledge, the defendant must make a reasonable investigation into whether the information exists and how difficult it would be to ascertain.
rules.short_rule_statement: The answer must admit or deny the allegations of the plaintiff’s complaint. Rule 8(b). If the defendant lacks knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief concerning...
rules.source_text:
Admissions Or Denials
The answer must admit or deny the allegations of the plaintiff’s complaint. Rule 8(b). If the defendant lacks knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief concerning the truth or falsity of an allegation, then the defendant must say so in the answer. This response has the effect of a denial under Rule 8(b). Before pleading lack of sufficient knowledge, the defendant must make a reasonable investigation into whether the information exists and how difficult it would be to ascertain.

rules.rule_statement: A specific denial is a denial of a particular paragraph or allegation in the complaint or other claim for relief (e.g., counterclaim, crossclaim, etc.). A party can respond to each paragraph of the complaint by:

A specific denial is a denial of a particular paragraph or allegation in the complaint or other claim for relief (e.g., counterclaim, crossclaim, etc.). A party can respond to each paragraph of the complaint by:

rules.canonical_name: Denying The Allegation In The Paragraph
rules.rule_statement: ii) Admitting the allegation in the paragraph; iii) Pleading insufficient knowledge to either admit or deny it; or iv) Admitting part of the allegation and either denying or pleading insufficient knowledge concerning the rest.

rules.rule_statement: A general denial is a statement by a party that denies every allegation of the complaint. A general denial can be made only if the party intends in good faith to controvert all the allegations. Alternatively, a party could make a qualified general denial, stating that the party denies every allegation in the complaint, except certain specified allegations.

rules.source_text:
General Denial
A general denial is a statement by a party that denies every allegation of the complaint. A general denial can be made only if the party intends in good faith to controvert all the allegations. Alternatively, a party could make a qualified general denial, stating that the party denies every allegation in the complaint, except certain specified allegations.

rules.canonical_name: Effect Of A Failure To Deny
rules.rule_statement: An allegation, other than one relating to the amount of damages, will be deemed admitted if a responsive pleading is required and the allegation is not denied.

rules.rule_statement: If a defendant has a claim against the plaintiff, then the defendant may state it as a counterclaim in the answer to the complaint. Under certain circumstances, a counterclaim will be compulsory (see § VI.D.2. Counterclaims, infra) under Rule 13. The counterclaim must be pleaded or it will be precluded in any future litigation. A party may state separate claims as well as defenses, regardless of consistency. Rule 8(d).

Counterclaims
If a defendant has a claim against the plaintiff, then the defendant may state it as a counterclaim in the answer to the complaint. Under certain circumstances, a counterclaim will be compulsory (see § VI.D.2. Counterclaims, infra) under Rule 13. The counterclaim must be pleaded or it will be precluded in any future litigation. A party may state separate claims as well as defenses, regardless of consistency. Rule 8(d).

rules.rule_statement: If a motion is not made under Rule 12, then a defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint. Rule 12(a)(1)(A)(i). If the defendant has timely waived service under Rule 4(d), then the defendant must serve the answer within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. Rule 12(a)(1)(A) (ii).

If the defendant has timely waived service under Rule 4(d), then the defendant must serve the answer within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. rules.rule_statement: When a motion is made under Rule 12, a defendant will not have to serve an answer while the motion is pending. If the court denies or postpones disposition of the motion until a trial on the merits, then the answer must be served within 14 days after notice of the court’s action. Rule 12(a)(4)(A). If the court grants a motion for a more definite statement under Rule 12(e), then the answer must be served within 14 days after service of the more definite statement.

Motion Made Under Rule 12
When a motion is made under Rule 12, a defendant will not have to serve an answer while the motion is pending. If the court denies or postpones disposition of the motion until a trial on the merits, then the answer must be served within 14 days after notice of the court’s action. Rule 12(a)(4)(A). If the court grants a motion for a more definite statement under Rule 12(e), then the answer must be served within 14 days after service of the more definite statement.

rules.rule_statement: A reply is a response by the plaintiff to a defendant’s answer that is required only if ordered by the court. It can also be a response by a defendant to a plaintiff’s counterclaim answer, a third-party answer, or a crossclaim answer. A reply is made only when the court orders it. Rule 7(a)(7). In general, a party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply unless the order specifies a different time. Rule 12(a)(1)(C).

Reply
A reply is a response by the plaintiff to a defendant’s answer that is required only if ordered by the court. It can also be a response by a defendant to a plaintiff’s counterclaim answer, a third-party answer, or a crossclaim answer. A reply is made only when the court orders it. Rule 7(a)(7). In general, a party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply unless the order specifies a different time. Rule 12(a)(1)(C).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 15 provides the rules about when and how pleadings can be amended or supplemented.

Amendments And Supplemental Pleadings
Rule 15 provides the rules about when and how pleadings can be amended or supplemented.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 15(a), a party may amend a pleading once as of right no later than 21 days after serving it if no responsive pleading is required. If a responsive pleading is required, a party may amend within 21 days of (1) service of the responsive pleading or (2) being served with a motion under Rule 12(b), whichever is earlier. In practical terms, Rule 15 means that a plaintiff may amend the complaint even after being served with an answer (up to 21 days), but the plaintiff is also limited to 21 days to amend after being served with a Rule 12(b) motion. A party may amend a pleading during and after a trial if:

rules.source_text:
By Right
Under Rule 15(a), a party may amend a pleading once as of right no later than 21 days after serving it if no responsive pleading is required. If a responsive pleading is required, a party may amend within 21 days of (1) service of the responsive pleading or (2) being served with a motion under Rule 12(b), whichever is earlier. In practical terms, Rule 15 means that a plaintiff may amend the complaint even after being served with an answer (up to 21 days), but the plaintiff is also limited to 21 days to amend after being served with a Rule 12(b) motion. A party may amend a pleading during and after a trial if:

rules.rule_statement: The court should freely give leave to amend a pleading when justice so requires. Rule 15(a)(2). As a general matter, the court will first determine if the proposed amendment to the pleading would be futile because it would immediately be subject to dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6). If it would not, the amendment will generally be permitted unless it would result in undue prejudice to the opposing party. However, when the court has issued an order regarding the trial plan after a final pretrial conference, which may include the issues for trial, the court may modify that order only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(e).

By Leave Of The Court
The court should freely give leave to amend a pleading when justice so requires. Rule 15(a)(2). As a general matter, the court will first determine if the proposed amendment to the pleading would be futile because it would immediately be subject to dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6). If it would not, the amendment will generally be permitted unless it would result in undue prejudice to the opposing party. However, when the court has issued an order regarding the trial plan after a final pretrial conference, which may include the issues for trial, the court may modify that order only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(e).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 11 establishes the standards that attorneys and individual parties must meet when filing: Pleadings Motions Other papers filed with the court The Rule does not apply to mandatory disclosures and discovery requests, responses, and objections. Rule 11 provides for sanctions against parties, attorneys, and law firms for violations of the Rule. Mandatory disclosures and discovery requests, responses, and objections are subject to their own signature requirement (see § VII.B.6. Signature Requirement and Certification, infra) and enforcement provisions (see § VII.F. Enforcement, infra). Rule 11(d).

Rule 11

Mandatory disclosures and discovery requests, responses, and objections are subject to their own signature requirement (see § VII.B.6. Signature Requirement and Certification, infra) and enforcement provisions (see § VII.F. Enforcement, infra). Rule 11(d).

rules.rule_statement: The sanctions process can be initiated by a party’s motion or by the court on its own initiative. A motion for sanctions must be made separately from any other motion and must describe the specific conduct alleged to violate Rule 11. The motion must first be served on the opposing party under Rule 5. The opposing party must then be given 21 days to withdraw or correct the challenged pleading. If this 21-day “safe harbor” period passes, and the opposing party fails to withdraw or correct the pleading, the party seeking sanctions may file the motion with the court. Rule 11(c)(2). On its own initiative, the court may order an attorney, a law firm, or a party to show cause why conduct specifically described in the order has not violated Rule 11. Rule 11(c)(3).
rules.short_rule_statement: The sanctions process can be initiated by a party’s motion or by the court on its own initiative. A motion for sanctions must be made separately from any other motion and must...
rules.source_text:
How Initiated
The sanctions process can be initiated by a party’s motion or by the court on its own initiative. A motion for sanctions must be made separately from any other motion and must describe the specific conduct alleged to violate Rule 11. The motion must first be served on the opposing party under Rule 5. The opposing party must then be given 21 days to withdraw or correct the challenged pleading. If this 21-day “safe harbor” period passes, and the opposing party fails to withdraw or correct the pleading, the party seeking sanctions may file the motion with the court. Rule 11(c)(2). On its own initiative, the court may order an attorney, a law firm, or a party to show cause why conduct specifically described in the order has not violated Rule 11. Rule 11(c)(3).

rules.rule_statement: After notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court may, in its discretion, impose sanctions on attorneys, law firms, and parties for violations of Rule 11. Absent exceptional circumstances, a law firm must be held jointly responsible for a violation committed by its partner, associate, or employee. Rule 11(c)(1).
rules.short_rule_statement: After notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court may, in its discretion, impose sanctions on attorneys, law firms, and parties for violations of Rule 11. Absent...

After notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court may, in its discretion, impose sanctions on attorneys, law firms, and parties for violations of Rule 11. Absent exceptional circumstances, a law firm must be held jointly responsible for a violation committed by its partner, associate, or employee. Rule 11(c)(1).

Rule 11(c)(2).

ii) An order to pay a penalty to the court; or iii) If imposed on motion and warranted for effective deterrence, an order directing payment to the movant for part or the entirety of reasonable attorney’s fees and other expenses directly resulting from the violation. Rule 11(c)(2).

rules.rule_statement: Sanctions “must be limited to what suffices to deter repetition of the conduct or comparable conduct by others similarly situated.” Rule 11(c)(4). The court cannot impose a monetary sanction on its own unless it has issued an order to show cause. The order to show cause must be issued before the claims were voluntarily dismissed or settled by or against the party who is, or whose attorneys are, to be sanctioned. Rule 11(c)(5)(B). An order to show cause is a court order that requires a person to appear before the court to argue against the court’s intended action. The court also is not permitted to impose a monetary sanction against a represented party for violating the requirement that the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions of the paper be warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law. Rule 11(c)(5)(A).
rules.short_rule_statement: Sanctions “must be limited to what suffices to deter repetition of the conduct or comparable conduct by others similarly situated.” Rule 11(c)(4). The court cannot impose a...
rules.source_text:
Limitations
Sanctions “must be limited to what suffices to deter repetition of the conduct or comparable conduct by others similarly situated.” Rule 11(c)(4). The court cannot impose a monetary sanction on its own unless it has issued an order to show cause. The order to show cause must be issued before the claims were voluntarily dismissed or settled by or against the party who is, or whose attorneys are, to be sanctioned. Rule 11(c)(5)(B). An order to show cause is a court order that requires a person to appear before the court to argue against the court’s intended action. The court also is not permitted to impose a monetary sanction against a represented party for violating the requirement that the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions of the paper be warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law. Rule 11(c)(5)(A).

rules.rule_statement: An order imposing a sanction under Rule 11 must describe the sanctioned conduct and explain the basis for the sanction. Rule 11(c)(6).

Content Of Sanction Order

Joinder Of Parties

rules.rule_statement: Rule 20 provides the circumstances in which a plaintiff may join other plaintiffs in an action or in which defendants may be joined in the same action.

rules.source_text:
Permissive Joinder
Rule 20 provides the circumstances in which a plaintiff may join other plaintiffs in an action or in which defendants may be joined in the same action.

rules.rule_statement: The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).
rules.short_rule_statement: The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).
rules.source_text:
Extent Of Relief
The same relief need not be demanded among the joined plaintiffs or against the joined defendants. Rule 20(a)(3).

rules.rule_statement: To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any party. Rule 20(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any...
rules.source_text:
Protective Measures
To avoid unfairness or hardship to any party, the court may order separate trials on any claims joined or may make any other order to prevent delay or undue expense to any party. Rule 20(b).

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
rules.source_text:
Jurisdiction And Venue
A plaintiff or defendant sought to be joined must also meet the requirements of federal subject matter jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to defendants sought to be joined under the permissive joinder rule in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction. Example: While driving his car, Plaintiff, a citizen of Arizona, was hit by a truck operated by Defendant 1, a citizen of New Mexico. Plaintiff was thrown from his car and injured by a car driven by Defendant 2, a citizen of Nevada. Plaintiff brings a negligence suit for $100,000 against Defendant 1 in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Plaintiff then joins Defendant 2 under Rule 20, suing for $25,000. Under § 1367(b), there is no supplemental jurisdiction against Defendant 2. Nor is there diversity jurisdiction against Defendant 2 because the statutory jurisdictional amount ($75,000) is not met. ii)      Joinder of plaintiffs If multiple plaintiffs join together under Rule 20, then supplemental jurisdiction can exist for a claim that does not meet the statutory jurisdictional amount, provided the parties still meet the requirement of complete diversity. Example: Plaintiff 1 and Plaintiff 2, who are citizens of California, join together under Rule 20 as plaintiffs against Defendant, a citizen of Oregon, in a diversity suit for negligence. The claims arise out of the same occurrence or transaction. Plaintiff 1 claims $200,000 and Plaintiff 2 claims $25,000. Supplemental jurisdiction exists over Plaintiff 2’s claim even though the claim does not meet the statutory jurisdictional amount. But if Plaintiff 2 was a citizen of Oregon, there would not be complete diversity, and Plaintiff 2 would have to be dropped from the federal case. If a defendant is joined under Rule 20, then the court must have in personam jurisdiction over the defendant for joinder to be proper. Joinder under Rule 20 is also subject to any applicable venue requirements.

Joinder Of Defendants

rules.rule_statement: Rule 19 specifies the rules for joining additional parties. The requirements of jurisdiction (both subject matter and personal) and venue must still be met for compulsory joinder to occur. Under certain circumstances, if compulsory joinder cannot occur because of jurisdictional or venue issues, Rule 19(b) may require the action to be dismissed from federal court.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 19(b), if a necessary party cannot be joined because of jurisdictional or venue concerns, then the court must determine whether, in equity and good conscience, the action should proceed among the existing parties or be dismissed. Factors for the court to consider are: that person or the existing parties; ii) The extent to which any prejudice could be reduced or avoided by using protective provisions in the judgment, shaping the relief, or taking other measures; iii) Whether a judgment rendered in the person’s absence would be adequate; and iv) Whether the plaintiff would have an adequate remedy if the action were dismissed for nonjoinder.

rules.rule_statement: A claim of an intervenor must be supported by its own jurisdictional basis. Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to the claims of a person seeking to intervene as a plaintiff under Rule 24 (either as of right or permissively) in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction if the exercise of jurisdiction would be inconsistent with the requirements of diversity jurisdiction. Example: Party X, a citizen of Ohio, sues Party Y, a citizen of Michigan, in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Party Z, a citizen of Michigan, seeks to intervene as a plaintiff (as of right or permissively) under Rule 24. Party Z cannot intervene because Z would destroy diversity jurisdiction in the action. If Z were a citizen of any state other than Michigan, then Z could intervene, provided the requirements for intervention under Rule 24 were met.

Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A claim of an intervenor must be supported by its own jurisdictional basis. Under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction does not apply to the claims of a person seeking to intervene as a plaintiff under Rule 24 (either as of right or permissively) in a case based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction if the exercise of jurisdiction would be inconsistent with the requirements of diversity jurisdiction. Example: Party X, a citizen of Ohio, sues Party Y, a citizen of Michigan, in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction. Party Z, a citizen of Michigan, seeks to intervene as a plaintiff (as of right or permissively) under Rule 24. Party Z cannot intervene because Z would destroy diversity jurisdiction in the action. If Z were a citizen of any state other than Michigan, then Z could intervene, provided the requirements for intervention under Rule 24 were met.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential claimants to the property into a single lawsuit to determine who has a right to the property. There are two forms of interpleader, each with its own requirements: interpleader under Rule 22 and federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential...
rules.source_text:
Interpleader
Rule Interpleader allows a person holding property (traditionally known as the “stakeholder”) who legitimately fears multiple claims against the property to force all potential claimants to the property into a single lawsuit to determine who has a right to the property. There are two forms of interpleader, each with its own requirements: interpleader under Rule 22 and federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper even though the claims of the claimants, or the titles on which their claims depend, lack a common origin or are adverse and independent rather than identical. Also, joinder is proper even though the plaintiff denies liability in whole or in part to any or all of the claimants. A defendant who is exposed to similar liability may seek interpleader through a crossclaim or counterclaim. Rule 22(a).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper...
rules.source_text:
Basis
Under Rule 22, persons with claims that may expose a plaintiff to double or multiple liability may be joined as defendants and required to interplead. Such joinder is proper even though the claims of the claimants, or the titles on which their claims depend, lack a common origin or are adverse and independent rather than identical. Also, joinder is proper even though the plaintiff denies liability in whole or in part to any or all of the claimants. A defendant who is exposed to similar liability may seek interpleader through a crossclaim or counterclaim. Rule 22(a).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need federal question jurisdiction or, for diversity jurisdiction, the citizenship of the party bringing the action must be completely diverse from that of the claimants, and the statutory amount in controversy must be met. While the stakeholder needs to be diverse from the claimants, the claimants need not be diverse among themselves.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
Rule 22 does not create subject matter jurisdiction in interpleader actions. Rather, the court must already have jurisdiction over all parties. Therefore, a plaintiff will need federal question jurisdiction or, for diversity jurisdiction, the citizenship of the party bringing the action must be completely diverse from that of the claimants, and the statutory amount in controversy must be met. While the stakeholder needs to be diverse from the claimants, the claimants need not be diverse among themselves.

rules.rule_statement: An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.
rules.short_rule_statement: An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.
rules.source_text:
In Personam Jurisdiction
An interpleader action is an action against the claimants. Therefore, the general requirements of in personam jurisdiction in federal court must be met.

rules.rule_statement: Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.
rules.short_rule_statement: Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.
rules.source_text:
Venue
Federal venue requirements must also be satisfied in interpleader actions under Rule 22.

rules.rule_statement: There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.
rules.source_text:
Federal Statutory Interpleader
There are some key differences in the requirements for federal statutory interpleader under § 1335.

rules.rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to meet the $75,000 threshold required for regular diversity matters. The property at issue must merely be $500 or more in value.
rules.short_rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to...
rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
For statutory interpleader, diversity jurisdiction is met if any two adverse claimants are citizens of different states. However, the amount in controversy does not need to meet the $75,000 threshold required for regular diversity matters. The property at issue must merely be $500 or more in value.

rules.rule_statement: Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may affect the property that is subject to dispute. § 2361.
rules.short_rule_statement: Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may...
rules.source_text:
In Personam Jurisdiction
Statutory interpleader provides for nationwide personal jurisdiction and service of process and permits the federal court to enjoin other federal and state proceedings that may affect the property that is subject to dispute. § 2361.

rules.rule_statement: Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.
rules.short_rule_statement: Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.
rules.source_text:
Venue
Venue is proper in any federal judicial district where one of the claimants resides. § 1397.

rules.rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond in an appropriate amount.
rules.short_rule_statement: For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond...
rules.source_text:
Deposit
For statutory interpleader, unlike for interpleader under the Federal Rules, the stakeholder is required to either deposit with the court the property at issue or post a bond in an appropriate amount.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules) may join with it as many independent or alternative claims the party may have against an opposing party. Rule 18 permits joinder of claims, but it does not compel it. However, res judicata concerns will often require joinder. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules)...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 18(a), a party who can assert a claim, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim (i.e., a qualifying claim that clearly can be brought under the Federal Rules) may join with it as many independent or alternative claims the party may have against an opposing party. Rule 18 permits joinder of claims, but it does not compel it. However, res judicata concerns will often require joinder. See § IX.D. Claim Preclusion (Res Judicata), infra.

rules.rule_statement: A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages and a claim to set aside a conveyance that was fraudulent as a result of the defendant’s transfer of assets to frustrate enforcement of the claim for monetary damages.
rules.short_rule_statement: A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages...
rules.source_text:
Joinder Of Contingent Claims
A party may join two claims even though one of them is contingent on the disposition of the other. Rule 18(b). Example: A plaintiff may assert both a claim for monetary damages and a claim to set aside a conveyance that was fraudulent as a result of the defendant’s transfer of assets to frustrate enforcement of the claim for monetary damages.

rules.rule_statement: To join a claim under Rule 18, the court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the claim. EXAM NOTE: Remember that if subject matter jurisdiction is based on diversity jurisdiction, then a party may aggregate all claims joined against an opposing party to satisfy the statutory jurisdictional amount-in-controversy requirement.

Subject Matter Jurisdiction
To join a claim under Rule 18, the court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the claim. EXAM NOTE: Remember that if subject matter jurisdiction is based on diversity jurisdiction, then a party may aggregate all claims joined against an opposing party to satisfy the statutory jurisdictional amount-in-controversy requirement.

rules.rule_statement: If the original claim is based on federal question jurisdiction, then a nonfederal claim may be joined only if diversity jurisdiction exists or if the two claims are part of the same case or controversy as the federal claim such that supplemental jurisdiction applies.

rules.source_text:
Federal Question Jurisdiction
If the original claim is based on federal question jurisdiction, then a nonfederal claim may be joined only if diversity jurisdiction exists or if the two claims are part of the same case or controversy as the federal claim such that supplemental jurisdiction applies.

rules.rule_statement: The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18.
rules.short_rule_statement: The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18.

The venue requirements for federal court must be satisfied to join a claim under Rule 18.

rules.rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the reply to a counterclaim. Rule 13 governs the requirements for bringing a counterclaim. A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim (or crossclaim) within 21 days of service. Rule 12(a)(1)(B). EXAM NOTE: A federal court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the counterclaim.  Because a compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as does the original claim before the court, compulsory counterclaims generally fall under the supplemental jurisdiction of the federal court.  However, a permissive counterclaim must meet the requirements for diversity or federal question jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the...
rules.source_text:
Counterclaims
A counterclaim is a claim for relief made against an opposing party after an original claim has been made. A counterclaim may be asserted in the answer to the complaint and the reply to a counterclaim. Rule 13 governs the requirements for bringing a counterclaim. A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim (or crossclaim) within 21 days of service. Rule 12(a)(1)(B). EXAM NOTE: A federal court must have subject matter jurisdiction over the counterclaim.  Because a compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence as does the original claim before the court, compulsory counterclaims generally fall under the supplemental jurisdiction of the federal court.  However, a permissive counterclaim must meet the requirements for diversity or federal question jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: A pleading is required to state as a counterclaim any claim that, at the time of service, the pleader has against an opposing party if the claim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence that is the subject matter of the opposing party’s claim and does not require adding another party over whom the court cannot acquire jurisdiction. Rule 13(a)(1). However, the pleader is not required to make the claim if: pending action; or ii) The opposing party’s action is in rem or quasi in rem and the party does not assert any other counterclaim in that action.

rules.source_text:
Compulsory
A pleading is required to state as a counterclaim any claim that, at the time of service, the pleader has against an opposing party if the claim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence that is the subject matter of the opposing party’s claim and does not require adding another party over whom the court cannot acquire jurisdiction. Rule 13(a)(1). However, the pleader is not required to make the claim if:

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 13(b), a pleading may state as a counterclaim against an opposing party any claim that is not compulsory. Therefore, a party has discretion as to whether to raise the counterclaim in the action before the court or in a separate action.

rules.source_text:
Permissive
Under Rule 13(b), a pleading may state as a counterclaim against an opposing party any claim that is not compulsory. Therefore, a party has discretion as to whether to raise the counterclaim in the action before the court or in a separate action.

rules.rule_statement: A third-party defendant may file a counterclaim against either the original defendant or the original plaintiff. The requirements of Rule 13(a)–(b) govern whether a third-party defendant’s counterclaim is classified as compulsory or permissive. A third-party defendant is a party brought into a lawsuit by an original defendant based on a theory that the party may be liable for damages that the original defendant may owe to the original plaintiff. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a counterclaim may be joined as long as they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they are indispensable parties under Rule 19.

rules.source_text:
By Third Parties
A third-party defendant may file a counterclaim against either the original defendant or the original plaintiff. The requirements of Rule 13(a)–(b) govern whether a third-party defendant’s counterclaim is classified as compulsory or permissive. A third-party defendant is a party brought into a lawsuit by an original defendant based on a theory that the party may be liable for damages that the original defendant may owe to the original plaintiff. Under Rule 13(h), new parties to a counterclaim may be joined as long as they meet the requirements for joinder under Rule 20. New parties must be joined if they are indispensable parties under Rule 19.

rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim is a claim made against a co-party, such as a claim by a defendant against another defendant. The statement of a defense in a pleading that is labeled a crossclaim (e.g., an allegation that a codefendant is solely liable to the plaintiff) does not constitute a claim against a coparty. Under Rule 13(g), a pleading may state as a crossclaim any claim by one party against a coparty if: matter of the original action or of a counterclaim; or ii) The claim relates to any property that is the subject matter of the original action.

In General

rules.rule_statement: A crossclaim must fall within the subject matter jurisdiction of the federal court. This requirement is generally not a problem because, by definition, a crossclaim must arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim or it must relate to the property at issue. Therefore, crossclaims tend to fall under the court’s supplemental jurisdiction.

Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A crossclaim must fall within the subject matter jurisdiction of the federal court. This requirement is generally not a problem because, by definition, a crossclaim must arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the subject matter of the original action or of a counterclaim or it must relate to the property at issue. Therefore, crossclaims tend to fall under the court’s supplemental jurisdiction.

In Personam Jurisdiction And Venue
Because the parties are already before the court, personal jurisdiction is satisfied. Additionally, if venue was proper over the original claim, then a party cannot object to venue with regarding the crossclaim.

rules.rule_statement: Rule 14 provides the rules governing third-party claims (impleader). The court may sever any third-party claim if justice demands it. Impleaded claims are made by a defending party against a nonparty for all or part of the defending party’s liability on an original claim. The plaintiff may also assert related claims against the impleaded party. In both cases, the impleaded claim must relate to the original claim against the defending party.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 14 provides the rules governing third-party claims (impleader). The court may sever any third-party claim if justice demands it. Impleaded claims are made by a defending...
rules.source_text:
In General
Rule 14 provides the rules governing third-party claims (impleader). The court may sever any third-party claim if justice demands it. Impleaded claims are made by a defending party against a nonparty for all or part of the defending party’s liability on an original claim. The plaintiff may also assert related claims against the impleaded party. In both cases, the impleaded claim must relate to the original claim against the defending party.

rules.rule_statement: A defending party—including a plaintiff against whom a counterclaim has been asserted— may assert a third-party claim at any time after the complaint is filed. The defending party, referred to for impleader purposes as the “third-party plaintiff,” must serve a summons and third-party complaint on the nonparty (third-party defendant). The third-party plaintiff must obtain the court’s permission if the filing was more than 14 days after service of the original answer. Rule 14(a)(1). The third-party defendant may (and in some cases must) assert defenses, counterclaims, and crossclaims against any of the parties as appropriate under the Rules and may also implead another nonparty who is or may be liable to the third-party defendant for the claim on which the defendant was impleaded. Rule 14(a)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: A defending party—including a plaintiff against whom a counterclaim has been asserted— may assert a third-party claim at any time after the complaint is filed. The defending...
rules.source_text:
Procedure
A defending party—including a plaintiff against whom a counterclaim has been asserted— may assert a third-party claim at any time after the complaint is filed. The defending party, referred to for impleader purposes as the “third-party plaintiff,” must serve a summons and third-party complaint on the nonparty (third-party defendant). The third-party plaintiff must obtain the court’s permission if the filing was more than 14 days after service of the original answer. Rule 14(a)(1). The third-party defendant may (and in some cases must) assert defenses, counterclaims, and crossclaims against any of the parties as appropriate under the Rules and may also implead another nonparty who is or may be liable to the third-party defendant for the claim on which the defendant was impleaded. Rule 14(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: A third-party claim must fall within the federal court’s subject matter jurisdiction. Because, by definition, a third-party claim will be closely related to the original claim, the court generally will have supplemental jurisdiction over the matter. However, if the original claims are based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to claims by the plaintiff against a third-party defendant brought in under Rule 14. These claims need to meet diversity or federal question jurisdiction requirements on their own.

rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A third-party claim must fall within the federal court’s subject matter jurisdiction. Because, by definition, a third-party claim will be closely related to the original claim, the court generally will have supplemental jurisdiction over the matter. However, if the original claims are based exclusively on diversity jurisdiction under § 1367(b), supplemental jurisdiction will not apply to claims by the plaintiff against a third-party defendant brought in under Rule 14. These claims need to meet diversity or federal question jurisdiction requirements on their own.

rules.rule_statement: Personal jurisdiction must exist over the third-party defendant for impleader to apply. Third parties joined by impleader may be served within 100 miles of where the summons was issued even if service takes place outside of the state and beyond its long-arm statute jurisdiction. Rule 4(k)(1)(B).

rules.rule_statement: In a class action, the court authorizes a single person or a small group of people to represent the interests of a larger group. Rule 23 and the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 govern class actions.

rules.source_text:
Class Actions
In a class action, the court authorizes a single person or a small group of people to represent the interests of a larger group. Rule 23 and the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 govern class actions.

rules.rule_statement: No strict threshold exists for determining whether the requirement of “numerosity” has been met. However, a class of 40 or more members is generally presumed to be so numerous that joinder of all members is impracticable, while a class of fewer than 20 members is unlikely to be certified absent other indications of impracticability of joinder.

Three Types
In addition to the requirements of Rule 23(a), before a class action can be certified, it must fit within one of the three situations specified in Rule 23(b). Under Rule 23(b)(1), the class is maintainable if the prosecution of separate actions:

rules.canonical_name: Would Create The Risk That The Class Opponent Would Become Subject To
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 23(b)(2), a class seeking final injunctive or declaratory relief may be certified if the class shares a general claim against the opposing party. An additional claim for monetary damages may not be sought when the monetary relief is not incidental to the injunctive or declaratory relief. Moreover, a claim for individualized monetary relief is not available because a claim under Rule 23(b)(2) is available only when a single, indivisible remedy would provide relief to each class member. Under Rule 23(b)(3), a class can be certified if questions of law or fact that are common to the class members predominate over any questions affecting only individual members. In these circumstances, the class action must be the superior method for bringing about a fair and efficient adjudication of the controversy. In making this determination, the court must consider, among other things: defense of separate actions; ii) The extent and nature of any litigation concerning the controversy already begun by or against class members; iii) The desirability or undesirability of concentrating the litigation of the claims in the particular forum; and iv) The likely difficulties in managing a class action.

rules.rule_statement: A class action must also satisfy federal subject matter jurisdiction requirements. A class action can invoke federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction. If diversity jurisdiction is invoked in a class action, then diversity of citizenship will be satisfied if the class representatives are diverse from the party or parties opposing the claim. When at least one representative plaintiff of a putative class action has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount (generally it must exceed $75,000), other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction.

rules.source_text:
Subject Matter Jurisdiction
A class action must also satisfy federal subject matter jurisdiction requirements. A class action can invoke federal question jurisdiction or diversity jurisdiction. If diversity jurisdiction is invoked in a class action, then diversity of citizenship will be satisfied if the class representatives are diverse from the party or parties opposing the claim. When at least one representative plaintiff of a putative class action has a claim that meets the statutory jurisdictional amount (generally it must exceed $75,000), other persons with claims that do not meet the jurisdictional amount can be made part of the class under the doctrine of supplemental jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: When the class action involves a defendant class, venue is determined based on the residences of the representative parties, not the residences of all the class members.

rules.rule_statement: Under CAFA, any defendant can remove the case to federal court. If the class action claim is based only on federal securities law or corporate governance, then there is no federal jurisdiction under CAFA. This exclusion also applies to primary defendants who are government entities. The benefit to filing under CAFA is the various protections afforded to the class members. The court will generally protect members against loss in consumer class actions, oversee that the settlement is fair to the members, and ensure that the settlement is equally distributed. The court will also notify federal and state officials of the proposed settlement. If notice is not given, then a class member may choose not to be bound by the agreement. The court is required to repudiate jurisdiction when:

The parties seeking approval must file a statement identifying any collateral agreements made related to the proposal.

Standard For Approval
If the proposal would bind class members, then the court may approve it only after a hearing and on finding that the proposal is fair, reasonable, and adequate. The parties seeking approval must file a statement identifying any collateral agreements made related to the proposal.

rules.rule_statement: Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over the objections of a class member who was not a named representative, the objecting class member’s interests diverge from those of the class representatives, and the class member who objects may appeal the court’s approval of a settlement.
rules.short_rule_statement: Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over...
rules.source_text:
Objection By Class Members
Any class member may object to the proposal if it requires court approval. Rule 23(e)(5). Once the named representative class members reach a settlement that is approved over the objections of a class member who was not a named representative, the objecting class member’s interests diverge from those of the class representatives, and the class member who objects may appeal the court’s approval of a settlement.

rules.rule_statement: If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the litigation and proceed on their own. Rule 23(e)(4). A class member who fails to opt out is bound by the settlement.
rules.short_rule_statement: If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the...
rules.source_text:
Second Opt-Out Opportunity
If a class action was certified under Rule 23(b)(3), the court may refuse to approve of the settlement unless class members are given a second opportunity to opt out of the litigation and proceed on their own. Rule 23(e)(4). A class member who fails to opt out is bound by the settlement.

rules.rule_statement: In general, a valid judgment binds all members of the class. In a class action certified under Rule 23(b)(3), a valid judgment does not bind those class members who opted out of the lawsuit. However, a class member who does not opt out is bound by the judgment even when that class member did not have minimum contacts with the forum state. 1 Newberg on Class Actions § 3:12 (6th ed.); see Gen. Tel. Co. of the Nw. v. Equal Emp. Opportunity Comm’n, 446 U.S. 318 (1980). Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011) (a claim for backpay due to alleged gender discrimination could not be certified in an action seeking injunction and declaratory relief for such discrimination). Amgen Inc. v. Conn. Ret. Plans & Tr. Funds, 568 U.S. 455 (2013). Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Allapattah Servs., Inc., 545 U.S. 546 (2005). Eisen v. Carlisle & Jacquelin, 417 U.S. 156 (1974). Devlin v. Scardelletti, 536 U.S. 1 (2002); Marino v. Ortiz, 484 U.S. 301 (1988). Phillips Petroleum Co. v. Shutts, 472 U.S. 797 (1985).

Judgment
In general, a valid judgment binds all members of the class. In a class action certified under Rule 23(b)(3), a valid judgment does not bind those class members who opted out of the lawsuit. rules.rule_statement: A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to enforce. While not generally subject to the Rules for a class action, the shareholder must represent the shareholders of the corporation fairly and adequately. The shareholder is bound to plead with particularity that a demand was made on the directors, and, if state law requires, also made a demand on the shareholders. If a demand was not made, the shareholder must state why. The shareholder must also assert status as a shareholder at the time the transaction occurred or that this status was subsequently gained by operation of law (e.g., by inheritance). Finally, the shareholder must allege that the action is not a collusive effort to impart jurisdiction on the court. Rule 23.1.
rules.short_rule_statement: A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to...
rules.source_text:
Shareholder Derivative Suits
A shareholder derivative action is an action brought by a shareholder on behalf of a corporation to enforce a right of the corporation that the corporation has failed to enforce. While not generally subject to the Rules for a class action, the shareholder must represent the shareholders of the corporation fairly and adequately. The shareholder is bound to plead with particularity that a demand was made on the directors, and, if state law requires, also made a demand on the shareholders. If a demand was not made, the shareholder must state why. The shareholder must also assert status as a shareholder at the time the transaction occurred or that this status was subsequently gained by operation of law (e.g., by inheritance). Finally, the shareholder must allege that the action is not a collusive effort to impart jurisdiction on the court. Rule 23.1.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 26(a) requires the parties to make certain disclosures: Initial disclosures Disclosures of expert testimony 90 days before trial Pretrial disclosures 30 days before trial The three sets of disclosures are mandatory and must be made even if an opposing party does not ask for such information. Unless otherwise ordered by the court, the disclosures must be in writing, signed, and served.

Mandatory Disclosures
Rule Rule 26(a) requires the parties to make certain disclosures: Initial disclosures Disclosures of expert testimony 90 days before trial Pretrial disclosures 30 days before trial The three sets of disclosures are mandatory and must be made even if an opposing party does not ask for such information. Unless otherwise ordered by the court, the disclosures must be in writing, signed, and served.

rules.rule_statement: Unless otherwise agreed by stipulation or ordered by the court, each party must provide to the other parties: likely to have discoverable information, along with the subjects of that information that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; ii) A copy, or a description by category and location, of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; iii) A computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party, who must also make available for inspection and copying the documents or other evidentiary material on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injury, unless privileged or protected from disclosure; and iv) For inspection and copying, any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment.

rules.rule_statement: A party must make these disclosures about experts at the times and in the sequence that the court orders. Absent a stipulation or court order, the disclosures must be made at least 90 days before the date set for trial or for the case to be ready for trial. If the evidence is intended solely to contradict or rebut the opposing party’s expert evidence on the same subject matter, then disclosure must be made within 30 days after the other party’s disclosure. Rule 26(a)(2)(D).

rules.source_text:
Time To Disclose Expert Testimony
A party must make these disclosures about experts at the times and in the sequence that the court orders. Absent a stipulation or court order, the disclosures must be made at least 90 days before the date set for trial or for the case to be ready for trial. If the evidence is intended solely to contradict or rebut the opposing party’s expert evidence on the same subject matter, then disclosure must be made within 30 days after the other party’s disclosure. Rule 26(a)(2)(D).

rules.rule_statement: The parties must supplement these disclosures when required under Rule 26(e). (See § VII.B.8. Supplementation, infra.) For an expert whose report must be disclosed, the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Any additions or changes to this information must be disclosed by the time that the party’s pretrial disclosures under Rule 26(a)(3) are due.

Supplementing The Disclosure
The parties must supplement these disclosures when required under Rule 26(e). (See § VII.B.8. Supplementation, infra.) For an expert whose report must be disclosed, the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Any additions or changes to this information must be disclosed by the time that the party’s pretrial disclosures under Rule 26(a)(3) are due.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(a)(3)(A), in addition to the initial and expert disclosures, the parties must make certain disclosures regarding evidence that they may present at trial other than for impeachment purposes. They must file with the court: each witness, separately identifying those the party expects to present and those it may call if the need arises; ii) The designation of those witnesses whose testimony the party expects to present by deposition and, if not taken stenographically, a transcript of the pertinent parts of the deposition; and iii) An identification of each document or other exhibit, including summaries of other evidence, separately identifying those items the party expects to offer and those it may offer if the need arises.
