rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a variety of discovery methods provided in Rules 27–36. When litigation is reasonably anticipated, potential litigants in possession of potentially relevant evidence have a duty to preserve such evidence. To trigger a duty to preserve evidence, the reasonable anticipated future litigation must be probable—more than a mere possibility. Once a duty to preserve evidence is triggered, the party in possession of the evidence must take reasonable measures to preserve it.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a...
rules.source_text:
Discovery Scope And Limits
Rule Rule 26 provides rules establishing the scope and limitations of discovery. In addition to obtaining information under the mandatory disclosure rules, parties may use a variety of discovery methods provided in Rules 27–36. When litigation is reasonably anticipated, potential litigants in possession of potentially relevant evidence have a duty to preserve such evidence. To trigger a duty to preserve evidence, the reasonable anticipated future litigation must be probable—more than a mere possibility. Once a duty to preserve evidence is triggered, the party in possession of the evidence must take reasonable measures to preserve it.

In diversity or supplemental claims in which a state’s substantive laws apply, that state’s law determines whether a privilege applies.

Privilege
Privileged information is not discoverable. In federal question cases, privileges are determined under federal common law, pursuant to Rule 501 of the Federal Rules of Evidence. In diversity or supplemental claims in which a state’s substantive laws apply, that state’s law determines whether a privilege applies.

rules.rule_statement: A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or cost. If challenged, the party will have the burden to show the undue burden and cost. Even if undue burden and cost are shown, the court may still order such discovery if the requesting party shows good cause. Rule 26(b)(2)(B). Moreover, the requesting party may specify the form in which the documents should be produced.
rules.short_rule_statement: A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or...
rules.source_text:
Limitations On Electronically Stored Information
A party is not required to provide discovery of electronically stored information from sources that the party identifies as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or cost. If challenged, the party will have the burden to show the undue burden and cost. Even if undue burden and cost are shown, the court may still order such discovery if the requesting party shows good cause. Rule 26(b)(2)(B). Moreover, the requesting party may specify the form in which the documents should be produced.

rules.rule_statement: Spoliation of evidence is the negligent or intentional destruction or significant alteration of evidence required for discovery. Spoliation of evidence may result in sanctions under Rule 37, as well as liability under an independent tort cause of action. Sanctions are authorized under Rule 37 only if the information cannot be restored or replaced by additional discovery. When retrieval of the information is possible, a court may order retrieval and assign the costs to the party who destroyed the evidence, and no further sanctions may be imposed. Rules 26(b)(2)(B) and Committee Notes on Rules—2015 Amendments to Rule 37(e). If a party has a policy in place that results in routine operations that may destroy evidence in the regular course of business, that party has a duty to affirmatively act to prevent the destruction or alteration of such evidence. Failure to do so may result in the negligent destruction of evidence. Sanctions for the negligent loss of electronically stored information may be no greater than necessary to cure the prejudice. Rule 37(e). If the court finds that the sanctioned party acted with the purpose of depriving the other party of the evidence’s use in litigation, then the available sanctions include: sanctioned party; ii) A jury instruction that it may (or must) presume the information was unfavorable to the party; or iii) An entry of a default judgment against the party.

When retrieval of the information is possible, a court may order retrieval and assign the costs to the party who destroyed the evidence, and no further sanctions may be imposed. Trial-Preparation Materials
Rule 26(b)(3) provides limited protection for otherwise discoverable trial-preparation materials or an attorney’s work product. In general, a party may not discover documents and tangible things that are prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial by or for another party or its representative. Under Rule 26(b)(4)(A), a party is entitled to depose any expert witness of an opposing party whose opinions may be presented at trial. Rule 26(b)(3) protects drafts of any expert report or disclosure required under Rule 26(a)(2). See § VII.A.2. Disclosure of Expert Testimony, supra.

For example, a party may seek a protective order limiting the time and manner of conducting a physical examination.

rules.source_text:
Protective Orders
Under Rule 26(c), for good cause, the court may enter protective orders to protect parties and other persons from annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or expense resulting from discovery. For example, a party may seek a protective order limiting the time and manner of conducting a physical examination.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 26(e)(1)(A), if a party who made a required disclosure or responded to an interrogatory, a request for production, or a request for admission learns that their disclosure or response is materially incomplete or incorrect, then the party is required to supplement or correct the disclosure or response in a timely manner. There is no duty to supplement or correct if the information has otherwise been made known to the other parties in discovery or in writing. Regarding an expert whose report must be disclosed under Rule 26(a)(2)(B), the party’s duty to supplement extends both to information included in the expert’s report and to information given during the expert’s deposition. Rule 26(e)(2). See Silvestri v. Gen’l Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001). Eckstein Marine Serv., Inc. v. M/V Basin Pride, 168 F.R.D. 38 (W.D. La. 1996). Gehring v. Case Corp., 43 F.3d 340 (7th Cir. 1994). Doe v. Norwalk Community College, 248 F.R.D. 372 (D. Conn. 2007).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 30, a party may take the deposition of any party or nonparty witness at any time after the discovery conference pursuant to Rule 26(f). A party who seeks an oral deposition of a person must give reasonable written notice to every other party, stating the time and place of the deposition and, if known, the deponent’s name and address. Notice of deposition is all that is needed to compel attendance by any party, even a party who is beyond the reach of the court’s subpoena power. To compel attendance by a nonparty, a subpoena must be served. Rule 30(b). entity When deposing a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, Rule 30(b)(6) states that the notice must describe with reasonable particularity the areas of inquiry. Additionally, the notice must state that the named entity has the duty to designate a representative regarding the designated areas of inquiry. The named entity must then designate one or more officers, directors, managing agents, or other persons who consent to testify on the entity’s behalf concerning those areas. Before or promptly after the notice or subpoena is served, the serving party and the organization must confer in good faith about the matters for examination. A subpoena must advise a nonparty organization of its duty to confer with the serving party. Without leave of the court, the plaintiffs, the defendants, and the third-party defendants, each as a group, are limited to 10 depositions by oral or written examination. Unless the parties agree to the deposition, leave of the court must be obtained to:

Like an oral deposition, a written deposition is subject to the rules regarding leave of the court (e.g., exception to 10-deposition rule) and deposing a corporation or an organization. Written depositions must also comply with these requirements: notice, identifying the deponent and the officer before whom the deposition will be taken, to all other parties.

rules.rule_statement: Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice when there are irregularities with the notice and its service. In noting errors regarding the form of the written questions, the party submitting the questions should receive notice within five days after service of the last questions authorized. A seasonable objection must be made if there are errors in the manner of taking the deposition. A motion to suppress is required when there are errors related to the signing, certification, and transmittal of depositions.
rules.short_rule_statement: Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice...
rules.source_text:
Irregularities In Depositions
Certain errors and irregularities in depositions are waived if not objected to in a timely manner. There must be a written objection promptly served on the party giving notice when there are irregularities with the notice and its service. In noting errors regarding the form of the written questions, the party submitting the questions should receive notice within five days after service of the last questions authorized. A seasonable objection must be made if there are errors in the manner of taking the deposition. A motion to suppress is required when there are errors related to the signing, certification, and transmittal of depositions.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses.
rules.source_text:
Availability
Under Rule 33(a), any party may serve no more than 25 written interrogatories on any other party. Interrogatories may not be used on nonparty witnesses.

rules.rule_statement: Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case. Rule 33(a)(2). An interrogatory is not objectionable merely because it asks for an opinion or contention that relates to fact or the application of law to fact. However, the court may order that the interrogatory need not be answered until designated discovery is complete or until a pretrial conference or some other time.
rules.short_rule_statement: Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the...
rules.source_text:
Scope
Interrogatories may relate to any matters permitted under Rule 26(b)(1)—nonprivileged matters relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case. Rule 33(a)(2). An interrogatory is not objectionable merely because it asks for an opinion or contention that relates to fact or the application of law to fact. However, the court may order that the interrogatory need not be answered until designated discovery is complete or until a pretrial conference or some other time.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, the interrogatories must be answered by any officer or agent who must furnish the information available to the party. The person who makes the answers must sign them. Rule 33(b)(5). Each interrogatory must be answered fully and separately under oath unless the responding party objects to the interrogatory. A responding party must serve its answers (and any objections) within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government...
rules.source_text:
Answers
Under Rule 33(b)(1), the interrogatories must be answered by the party to whom they are directed. If that party is a corporation, partnership, association, or government entity, the interrogatories must be answered by any officer or agent who must furnish the information available to the party. The person who makes the answers must sign them. Rule 33(b)(5). Each interrogatory must be answered fully and separately under oath unless the responding party objects to the interrogatory. A responding party must serve its answers (and any objections) within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure for good cause. Rule 33(b)(2), (3). The attorney who objects must sign any objections. Rule 33(b)(5). Like an answer, any objections must be served by the responding party within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure...
rules.source_text:
Objections
The grounds for objecting to an interrogatory must be stated with specificity. Any ground not stated in a timely objection is deemed waived unless the court excuses the failure for good cause. Rule 33(b)(2), (3). The attorney who objects must sign any objections. Rule 33(b)(5). Like an answer, any objections must be served by the responding party within 30 days after being served with the interrogatories. The court may order or the parties may stipulate to a shorter or longer time. Rule 33(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: Rule 33(d) provides a mechanism for a responding party to answer an interrogatory by producing business records. This mechanism can be used when an answer to an interrogatory may be ascertained from (1) the responding party’s business records (including electronically stored information) or (2) a summation of the responding party’s business records. To exercise this option, the burden of deriving answers must be substantially the same for the party serving the interrogatories as for the responding party. Under this option, the responding party may answer the interrogatory by:

Option To Produce Business Records

To exercise this option, the burden of deriving answers must be substantially the same for the party serving the interrogatories as for the responding party. Under this option, the responding party may answer the interrogatory by:

rules.rule_statement: Rule 34 provides a party with a right to issue discovery to another party for the inspection of documents, tangible things, and land. Although these discovery requests may be directed only to other parties, nonparties may be compelled to produce documents and other things or submit to an inspection pursuant to a subpoena served under Rule 45. Rule 34(c).

rules.source_text:
In General
Rule 34 provides a party with a right to issue discovery to another party for the inspection of documents, tangible things, and land. Although these discovery requests may be directed only to other parties, nonparties may be compelled to produce documents and other things or submit to an inspection pursuant to a subpoena served under Rule 45. Rule 34(c).

rules.rule_statement: A party may serve on any other party a request to produce documents and tangible things in the responding party’s possession, custody, or control. Rule 34(a)(1). rules.rule_statement: Generally, the party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served, unless a shorter or longer time is stipulated to by the parties or ordered by court. If the request to produce was served before the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference, the responding party must respond within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. This time limitation may be shortened or expanded by stipulation or court order. If a party fails to respond to a document request or to allow inspection, or objects to a request, then the requesting party may move to compel under Rule 37(a). Rule 34(b) (2)(A). The responding party may state that it will produce copies of documents or electronically stored information instead of permitting inspection. The production must be completed no later than the time for inspection specified in the request or another reasonable time specified in the response. Rule 34(b)(2)(B). If a request does not specify a form for the production of electronically stored information, then a party must produce it in a form or forms in which it is ordinarily maintained or in a reasonably usable form or forms. A party need not produce the same electronically stored information in more than one form. Rule 34(b)(2)(E).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or mental examination by a “suitably licensed or certified examiner.” Rule 35(a) also permits the examination of a person in the legal custody or control of a party. A suitably licensed or certified examiner includes physicians and other professionals licensed to report on physical or mental conditions, such as dentists and psychologists.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 35(a), if the mental or physical condition (including blood group) of a party is in controversy, then the court may order that person to submit to a physical or mental examination by a “suitably licensed or certified examiner.” Rule 35(a) also permits the examination of a person in the legal custody or control of a party. A suitably licensed or certified examiner includes physicians and other professionals licensed to report on physical or mental conditions, such as dentists and psychologists.

rules.rule_statement: An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause. The person to be examined and all parties must be given prior notice specifying the time, place, conditions, and scope of the examination and the identity of the examiner. Rule 35(a) (2).
rules.short_rule_statement: An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause....
rules.source_text:
Procedure
An order for a physical or mental examination of a party (or someone in the party’s custody or control) may be made only upon motion. The requesting party must show good cause. The person to be examined and all parties must be given prior notice specifying the time, place, conditions, and scope of the examination and the identity of the examiner. Rule 35(a) (2).

rules.rule_statement: On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include results of all tests, diagnoses, and conclusions as well as any reports of earlier examinations of the same condition. If the examiner refuses to make a report, then the examiner’s testimony may be excluded at trial. Rule 35(b).
rules.short_rule_statement: On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include...
rules.source_text:
The Examiner’S Report
On request by the party or person examined, the party who requested the examination must provide a copy of the examiner’s written report. The examiner’s report must include results of all tests, diagnoses, and conclusions as well as any reports of earlier examinations of the same condition. If the examiner refuses to make a report, then the examiner’s testimony may be excluded at trial. Rule 35(b).

rules.rule_statement: After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent examinations of the same condition in that party’s custody or control. Rule 35(b)(3). If the party who was examined obtains the report of the examination or conducts a deposition of the examiner, then the party waives any privilege it may have regarding the testimony of any other person who has examined or subsequently examines the party concerning the same condition. Rule 35(b)(4).
rules.short_rule_statement: After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent...
rules.source_text:
Other Examinations
After delivery of the examiner’s report, the examined party must provide the party who moved for the examination, on request, a report of all other previous or subsequent examinations of the same condition in that party’s custody or control. Rule 35(b)(3). If the party who was examined obtains the report of the examination or conducts a deposition of the examiner, then the party waives any privilege it may have regarding the testimony of any other person who has examined or subsequently examines the party concerning the same condition. Rule 35(b)(4).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested matters may relate to statements or opinions of fact or to the application of law to fact, including the genuineness of any documents described in the request. Rule 36(a)(1). Each matter for which an admission is requested must be specified separately. A request to admit the genuineness of a document must be accompanied by a copy of the document unless it is, or has been, otherwise furnished or made available for inspection and copying. Rule 36(a)(2).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested...
rules.source_text:
In General
Under Rule 36, a party may serve upon any other party a written request for the admission of any relevant, nonprivileged matters discoverable under Rule 26. The requested matters may relate to statements or opinions of fact or to the application of law to fact, including the genuineness of any documents described in the request. Rule 36(a)(1). Each matter for which an admission is requested must be specified separately. A request to admit the genuineness of a document must be accompanied by a copy of the document unless it is, or has been, otherwise furnished or made available for inspection and copying. Rule 36(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: A matter will be admitted unless the responding party serves the requesting party with a written answer or objection addressed to the matter. A response to a request for admissions must be served within 30 days after being served with the request and must be signed by the party or her attorney. Rule 36(a)(3). If a matter is not admitted, then the answer must specifically deny it or state in detail why the answering party cannot truthfully admit or deny it. A denial must fairly respond to the substance of the matter. When good faith requires that a party qualify an answer or deny only a part of a matter, the answer must specify the part admitted and qualify or deny the rest. Rule 36(a)(4). An answering party may assert lack of knowledge or information as a reason for failing to admit or deny only if the party states:

rules.source_text:
Responses And Objections
A matter will be admitted unless the responding party serves the requesting party with a written answer or objection addressed to the matter. A response to a request for admissions must be served within 30 days after being served with the request and must be signed by the party or her attorney. Rule 36(a)(3). If a matter is not admitted, then the answer must specifically deny it or state in detail why the answering party cannot truthfully admit or deny it. A denial must fairly respond to the substance of the matter. When good faith requires that a party qualify an answer or deny only a part of a matter, the answer must specify the part admitted and qualify or deny the rest. Rule 36(a)(4). An answering party may assert lack of knowledge or information as a reason for failing to admit or deny only if the party states:

rules.rule_statement: If a party fails to make the automatic disclosures required by Rule 26(a) or fails to respond to discovery that has been properly served, then the party seeking the information may move to compel such disclosure or discovery. An evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response is treated as a failure to disclose, answer, or respond. Rule 37(a).

rules.source_text:
In General
If a party fails to make the automatic disclosures required by Rule 26(a) or fails to respond to discovery that has been properly served, then the party seeking the information may move to compel such disclosure or discovery. An evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response is treated as a failure to disclose, answer, or respond. Rule 37(a).

rules.rule_statement: A motion to compel must be served on all parties and be accompanied by a certificate that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the opposing party in an effort to obtain the disclosure or secure the information or material without court action. Rule 37(a)(1). If the subject of the motion to compel is a party, then the motion must be filed with the court in which the action is pending. If the subject of the motion is a nonparty who was subpoenaed under Rule 45, then the motion must be filed in the court that issued the subpoena. Rule 37(a)(2).

rules.source_text:
Procedure
A motion to compel must be served on all parties and be accompanied by a certificate that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the opposing party in an effort to obtain the disclosure or secure the information or material without court action. Rule 37(a)(1). If the subject of the motion to compel is a party, then the motion must be filed with the court in which the action is pending. If the subject of the motion is a nonparty who was subpoenaed under Rule 45, then the motion must be filed in the court that issued the subpoena. Rule 37(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: A successful movant is entitled to recover the movant’s reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred in connection with the motion. The court may order the opposing party, the party’s attorney who advised the refusal of disclosure or discovery, or both to pay the movant’s expenses. Rule 37(a)(5)(A). Expenses cannot be awarded if: before filing the motion; ii) The opposing party demonstrates that the nondisclosure, response, or objection was substantially justified; or iii) Other circumstances render an award of expenses unjust.

rules.rule_statement: If the motion to compel is denied and the court determines that the motion was made without substantial justification, then, after providing an opportunity to be heard, the court must require the movant, the attorney filing the motion, or both to pay the party (or person) who opposed the motion the reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred in opposing the motion. Rule 37(a)(5)(B). If the motion is granted in part and denied in part, then the court may apportion fees in its discretion after providing an opportunity for the parties to be heard. Rule 37(a)(5) (C).

rules.canonical_name: Failure To Make Or Supplement Automatic Disclosures
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 37(c)(1), if a party fails to make or supplement its automatic disclosures as required by Rules 26(a) and (e), then the party will not be permitted to use the undisclosed documents or call the undisclosed witnesses unless the nondisclosure was substantially justified or was harmless. This Rule applies to the use of such evidence at trial and for motions and hearings. It does not apply to the use of such evidence for impeachment purposes because Rule 26(a) does not require disclosure of impeachment evidence or witnesses.

It does not apply to the use of such evidence for impeachment purposes because Rule 26(a) does not require disclosure of impeachment evidence or witnesses.

rules.rule_statement: Sanctions are subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. The abuse-of-discretion standard of review is a highly deferential standard of review under which an appellate court can reverse a trial judge’s discretionary ruling only if it was unreasonable or arbitrary. Generally, the harshest sanctions—dismissal of the action and entry of a default judgment— are reserved for misconduct that is serious, repeated, contumacious, extreme, or inexcusable. Generally, a court should impose lesser sanctions before issuing more severe ones. See Bachier-Ortiz v. Colon-Mendoza, 331 F.3d 193 (1st Cir. 2003).

Standard Of Review
Sanctions are subject to review under the abuse-of-discretion standard. The abuse-of-discretion standard of review is a highly deferential standard of review under which an appellate court can reverse a trial judge’s discretionary ruling only if it was unreasonable or arbitrary. Generally, the harshest sanctions—dismissal of the action and entry of a default judgment— are reserved for misconduct that is serious, repeated, contumacious, extreme, or inexcusable. Generally, a court should impose lesser sanctions before issuing more severe ones. See Bachier-Ortiz v. Colon-Mendoza, 331 F.3d 193 (1st Cir. 2003).

Additionally, the court may require that a party or its representative be present or reasonably available by telephone or by more sophisticated electronic means to consider possible settlement. Rule 16(c)(1). After a pretrial conference, the court should issue an order reciting the action taken. This order controls the course of the action unless the court later modifies it. Rule 16(d).

rules.rule_statement: The court may hold a final pretrial conference to formulate a trial plan, including a plan to facilitate the admission of evidence. The conference must be held as close to the start of the trial as is reasonable. The conference must be attended by at least one attorney who will conduct the trial for each party and by any unrepresented party. The court should issue an order reciting the action taken and may modify the order issued after a final pretrial conference only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(d), (e).

rules.source_text:
Final Pretrial Conference And Order
The court may hold a final pretrial conference to formulate a trial plan, including a plan to facilitate the admission of evidence. The conference must be held as close to the start of the trial as is reasonable. The conference must be attended by at least one attorney who will conduct the trial for each party and by any unrepresented party. The court should issue an order reciting the action taken and may modify the order issued after a final pretrial conference only to prevent manifest injustice. Rule 16(d), (e).

rules.rule_statement: If counsel or a party fails to appear at a pretrial or scheduling conference, fails to participate in good faith, or fails to obey a pretrial or scheduling conference order, then the court may generally impose the same sanctions as those permitted for failure of a party to comply with a discovery order, including contempt of court or dismissal of an action (see § VII.F.2. Sanctions, supra). Dismissal of an action is a severe sanction. Generally, dismissal is appropriate only when a party’s conduct is serious, repeated, extreme, and otherwise inexcusable. Instead of or in addition to any other sanction, the court must order the party, its attorney, or both to pay the reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred because of any noncompliance with this Rule, unless the noncompliance was substantially justified or other circumstances make an award of expenses unjust. Rule 16(f).

Instead of or in addition to any other sanction, the court must order the party, its attorney, or both to pay the reasonable expenses (including attorney’s fees) incurred because of any noncompliance with this Rule, unless the noncompliance was substantially justified or other circumstances make an award of expenses unjust. Dismissal
Rule 41 provides procedure for obtaining dismissal of a complaint, counterclaim, crossclaim, or third-party claim. Dismissal can be voluntary or involuntary under the Rule.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 41(a)(1)(A), a plaintiff may dismiss an action without leave of the court: either an answer or a motion for summary judgment; or ii) By filing a stipulation of dismissal signed by all parties who have appeared in the action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 41(a)(1)(A), a plaintiff may dismiss an action without leave of the court:
rules.source_text:
Voluntary
Under Rule 41(a)(1)(A), a plaintiff may dismiss an action without leave of the court:

rules.canonical_name: By Filing A Notice Of Dismissal At Any Time Before The Opposing Party Serves
rules.rule_statement: Unless otherwise stated in the notice or stipulation, the voluntary dismissal will be without prejudice. A dismissal is without prejudice when the plaintiff is permitted to sue the defendant on the same claim in the future. However, if a plaintiff voluntarily dismissed a prior state or federal action based on the same claim by filing a notice of dismissal, then a subsequent dismissal of the same claim by notice will be with prejudice. This is referred to as the two-dismissal rule. Rule 41(a)(1)(B). Except as provided previously, an action may be dismissed at the plaintiff’s request only by court order on terms that the court considers proper. Rule 41(a)(2). A voluntary dismissal by court order is without prejudice unless the order states otherwise. However, when a plaintiff moves for dismissal without prejudice under Rule 41(a)(2), the decision to dismiss with or without prejudice is left to the discretion of the court. Voluntary dismissal without prejudice is not a matter of right. The purpose of Rule 41(a)(2) is primarily to prevent voluntary dismissals that unfairly affect the defendant. If voluntary dismissal under Rule 41(a)(2) would unfairly affect the defendant, then the court may respond to a Rule 41(a)(2) motion to dismiss with an order dismissing the action with prejudice. However, if the court intends to do so, it must provide the plaintiff with notice of intent and an opportunity to respond or withdraw the motion.

If there is no responsive pleading, the voluntary dismissal must be made before evidence is introduced at a hearing or trial.

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 41(b), if the plaintiff fails to prosecute or to comply with the Rules or a court order, then a defendant may move to dismiss the action or any claim against him. Unless the court’s dismissal order specifies otherwise, a dismissal under Rule 41(b) is with prejudice and operates as an adjudication on the merits. However, a dismissal based on a lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or failure to join an indispensable party under Rule 19 does not operate as an adjudication on the merits.

However, a dismissal based on a lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or failure to join an indispensable party under Rule 19 does not operate as an adjudication on the merits.

Default And Default Judgment
Defaults and default judgments are considered drastic remedies and are strongly disfavored by the courts.

rules.rule_statement: When a party has failed to plead or otherwise defend an action and that failure is shown by affidavit or otherwise, the court clerk must enter the party’s default. Rule 55(a).

Default
When a party has failed to plead or otherwise defend an action and that failure is shown by affidavit or otherwise, the court clerk must enter the party’s default. Rule 55(a).

rules.rule_statement: Once a default is entered against a party, the plaintiff may seek a default judgment. If the relief sought is a sum certain or an amount that can be made certain by computation, and the defendant who failed to appear is neither a minor nor an incompetent person, the default judgment must be entered by the court clerk on the plaintiff’s request accompanied by an affidavit showing the amount due. Rule 55(b)(1). Otherwise, the plaintiff must apply to the court for a default judgment. If the party against whom a default judgment is sought has appeared personally or by a representative, that party or its representative must be served with written notice of the application at least seven days before a hearing is held. The court may conduct a hearing when it needs to:

Default Judgment

Rule 55(b)(1). Otherwise, the plaintiff must apply to the court for a default judgment. If the party against whom a default judgment is sought has appeared personally or by a representative, that party or its representative must be served with written notice of the application at least seven days before a hearing is held. The court may conduct a hearing when it needs to:

The movant has the burden of persuasion on a motion for summary judgment. Once the movant makes a prima facie showing that summary judgment is appropriate, the burden shifts to the opposing party to provide specific evidence showing the existence of a genuine issue of fact for trial.

rules.rule_statement: In deciding a motion for summary judgment, the court must consider the materials cited by the parties even if they are not presented in a form that is admissible at trial, as long as the facts contained in the submissions are admissible at trial. Examples of such materials include: (1) affidavits, (2) documents, (3) electronically stored information, (4) pleadings, (5) deposition transcripts, (6) interrogatory answers, (7) admissions, and (8) stipulations filed by the parties. The court is not limited to the materials cited by the parties and may also consider other materials in the record. Supporting and opposing affidavits must:

rules.rule_statement: A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the full relief requested by a motion for summary judgment, it may enter an order stating any material fact that is not genuinely in dispute and treating the fact as established in the case. Material facts subject to partial summary judgment include an item of damages or other relief. Rule 56(g).
rules.short_rule_statement: A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the...

Partial Summary Judgment
A partial summary judgment is a court’s judgment for one party and against another on one or more—but not all—issues without a full trial. Even if the court does not grant the full relief requested by a motion for summary judgment, it may enter an order stating any material fact that is not genuinely in dispute and treating the fact as established in the case. Material facts subject to partial summary judgment include an item of damages or other relief. Rule 56(g).

rules.rule_statement: Unless a different time is set by local rule, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. Rule 56(b).

Time For Making The Motion
Unless a different time is set by local rule, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. Rule 56(b).

rules.rule_statement: A grant of full summary judgment is a final disposition on the merits and is subject to appeal. In general, an order denying summary judgment is not a final disposition and is not subject to immediate appeal. Depending on the grounds for the denial, a party may be required to file a sufficiency-of-the-evidence claim in a Rule 50 motion for judgment as a matter of law to preserve the claim for appeal. Basis for denial Rule 50 motion to preserve appeal Factual grounds Required Legal grounds Not required

rules.rule_statement: A declaratory judgment is a ruling in which the court tells the parties to a dispute what their rights, responsibilities, or obligations are, without awarding damages or ordering the parties to do or refrain from doing anything. Parties generally seek a declaratory judgment to resolve uncertainty and to avoid the possibility of a future lawsuit. Under the federal Declaratory Judgment Act, a federal court may award declaratory relief in actions within the court’s original jurisdiction, with the exception of certain actions concerning taxes, bankruptcy, free trade, or drug patents. Most states have enacted statutes authorizing their courts to issue declaratory judgments. See Mobil Oil Corp. v. Advanced Env’t Recycling Techs., Inc., 203 F.R.D. 156 (D. Del. 2001); Jaskot v. Brown, 167 F.R.D. 372 (S.D.N.Y. 1996). See Mobil Oil Corp. v. Advanced Env’t Recycling Techs., Inc., 203 F.R.D. 156 (D. Del. 2001); see also Chess v. Nieport, 386 F. Supp. 312 (E.D. Cal. 1974). Mich. Surgery Inv., LLC v. Arman, 627 F.3d 572 (6th Cir. 2010); Jaramillo v. Burkhart, 59 F.3d 78 (8th Cir. 1995); Andes v. Versant Corp., 788 F.2d 1033 (4th Cir. 1986). ASX Inv. Corp. v. Newton, 183 F.3d 1265 (11th Cir. 1999); see In re Estate of Migliaccio, 436 F. Supp. 2d 1095 (C.D. Cal. 2006). See, e.g., Russell-Brown v. Jerry, 270 F.R.D. 654 (N.D. Fla. 2010). See, e.g., Hughes v. Holland, 320 F.2d 781 (D.C. Cir. 1963). See Lacy v. Sitel Corp., 227 F.3d 290, 292 (5th Cir. 2000), Berthelsen v. Kane, 907 F.2d 617, 620 (6th Cir. 1990). Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986). Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986); see Scott v. Harris, 550 U.S. 372, 380 (2007) (“When opposing parties tell two different stories, one of which is blatantly contradicted by the record, so that no reasonable jury could believe it, a court should not adopt that version of the facts for purposes of ruling on a motion for summary judgment.”). Stinnett v. Iron Works Gym/Executive Health Spa, Inc., 301 F.3d 610, 613 (7th Cir. 2002). 28 U.S.C. § 1746. See White’s Landing Fisheries v. Buchholzer, 29 F.3d 229 (6th Cir. 1994); Weir v. Anaconda Co., 773 F.2d 1073 (10th Cir. 1985). See, e.g., Ortiz v. Jordan, 562 U.S. 180 (2011). See, e.g., Dupree v. Younger, 598 U.S. 729 (2023). 28 U.S.C. § 2201.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the parties inviolate. Under Rule 38(b), any party may make a written demand for a trial by jury.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the...
rules.source_text:
Jury Trial
Rule Rule 38 provides that the right of trial by jury as declared by the Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, or as provided by a federal statute, is preserved to the parties inviolate. Under Rule 38(b), any party may make a written demand for a trial by jury.

rules.rule_statement: An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but an action in equity is not. If a new cause of action that was unknown at common law is created, then the court must look to the remedy sought and allow a jury trial if the relief sought is legal rather than equitable. In federal civil cases, the amount in controversy must exceed $20 for there to be a right to a jury trial.
rules.short_rule_statement: An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but...
rules.source_text:
Right To A Jury Trial
An action at law is an action for damages. An action in equity is typically an action for an injunctive relief. In general, an action at law is tried on demand to a jury, but an action in equity is not. If a new cause of action that was unknown at common law is created, then the court must look to the remedy sought and allow a jury trial if the relief sought is legal rather than equitable. In federal civil cases, the amount in controversy must exceed $20 for there to be a right to a jury trial.

rules.rule_statement: The right to trial by jury is evaluated for each claim. If an action involves both legal and equitable claims, then the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and the court then determines the equitable claims. In these cases, the court is bound by the jury’s findings on the legal claims.

Legal And Equitable Claims—Trial Order
The right to trial by jury is evaluated for each claim. If an action involves both legal and equitable claims, then the jury normally determines the legal claims first, and the court then determines the equitable claims. In these cases, the court is bound by the jury’s findings on the legal claims.

rules.rule_statement: With regard to state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law generally determines whether there is a right to a jury trial and whether an issue is legal or equitable. However, federal courts apply state law when considering a motion for a new trial based on excessiveness of the verdict.

Diversity Cases
With regard to state-law claims in diversity actions, federal law generally determines whether there is a right to a jury trial and whether an issue is legal or equitable. However, federal courts apply state law when considering a motion for a new trial based on excessiveness of the verdict.

rules.rule_statement: When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may be made separately or made in a pleading. The demand must be served within 14 days after service of the last pleading directed to the issue that is sought to be tried by a jury and filed with the court within a reasonable time after service. A party waives a jury trial unless the demand is properly served and filed.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may...
rules.source_text:
Jury Demand And Waiver
When a party makes a written demand for a trial by jury under Rule 38(b), the demand is filed, and the action must be designated on the docket as a jury action. The demand may be made separately or made in a pleading. The demand must be served within 14 days after service of the last pleading directed to the issue that is sought to be tried by a jury and filed with the court within a reasonable time after service. A party waives a jury trial unless the demand is properly served and filed.

rules.rule_statement: A party may specify the issues for which it is demanding a jury trial. Otherwise, a jury trial demand is treated as requesting a jury trial regarding all issues triable by a jury. If a party requests a jury trial for only some issues triable by a jury, then any other party may serve a jury trial demand on any or all remaining issues that are triable by a jury. This party must file its demand within 14 days of being served with the earlier jury trial demand or any shorter time set by the court. Rule 38(c). After a demand is filed, the trial on all issues demanded must be by jury unless:

If a party requests a jury trial for only some issues triable by a jury, then any other party may serve a jury trial demand on any or all remaining issues that are triable by a jury. This party must file its demand within 14 days of being served with the earlier jury trial demand or any shorter time set by the court. Rule 38(c). After a demand is filed, the trial on all issues demanded must be by jury unless:

Issues on which a jury trial is not properly demanded are to be tried by the court. However, on motion, the court may order a jury trial on any issue for which a jury might have been demanded. Rule 39(b).

rules.rule_statement: Even in actions in which there is no right to a jury trial, the court has discretion to authorize an advisory jury. The court may submit any issues of fact it chooses to the advisory jury and may either adopt or disregard all or part of the advisory jury’s findings. The findings of the advisory jury are not binding, and instructions given to the advisory jury are not subject to appellate review. Rule 39(c).

Advisory Jury
Even in actions in which there is no right to a jury trial, the court has discretion to authorize an advisory jury. The court may submit any issues of fact it chooses to the advisory jury and may either adopt or disregard all or part of the advisory jury’s findings. The findings of the advisory jury are not binding, and instructions given to the advisory jury are not subject to appellate review. Rule 39(c).

rules.rule_statement: A party may withdraw a jury trial demand with the consent of the other parties. Rule 38(d).

rules.canonical_name: Removal Of A Case From State Court
rules.rule_statement: If a case is removed from state court, a party who made a jury trial demand in accordance with state law need not renew the demand. If state law does not require a party to make a jury trial demand, then a party need not make one after removal unless the court orders the parties to do so. A party may request that the court make such an order, and the court must comply. If all necessary pleadings were filed before removal, a party entitled to a jury trial under Rule 38 may make a jury trial demand:

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 48(a), a jury must initially have at least 6 members and no more than 12 members. Once selected, a juror must participate in the verdict unless dismissed for good cause. No provision exists in the Rules for the selection of alternate jurors, as is the practice in some states.

rules.rule_statement: Each party may exercise three peremptory challenges in civil cases. Several coparties may be considered as a single party for the purposes of making those challenges. Alternatively, the court may allow additional peremptory challenges and permit them to be exercised separately or jointly. Crossover Topic: Criminal Procedure Peremptory challenges may not be made for racial or gender-based reasons. Challenges, regarding the rules for contesting a discriminatory use of a peremptory challenge. Each party is entitled to an unlimited number of challenges for cause, such as bias or a personal relationship with a litigant. The court rules on these challenges.

rules.rule_statement: If the jury is to render a general verdict, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to render a general verdict and answer the questions in writing. If the jury is to render specific written findings of fact, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to make its findings. Rule 49(a)(2), (b)(1).

Jury Instructions
If the jury is to render a general verdict, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to render a general verdict and answer the questions in writing. If the jury is to render specific written findings of fact, it is the judge’s duty to give the instructions and explanations necessary to enable the jury to make its findings. Rule 49(a)(2), (b)(1).

rules.canonical_name: Party’S Right To Request Speciﬁc Instructions
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 51, a party has the right to request that the court give specific instructions to the jury regarding its verdict. At the close of evidence, a party may file and furnish to every other party proposed jury instructions. The court may require that the party do this at an earlier reasonable time, including before trial. After the close of evidence, a party has the right to file requests for instructions on issues that could not reasonably have been anticipated at that earlier time. Additionally, the court may permit a party to file an untimely request. Rule 51(a).

The court may require that the party do this at an earlier reasonable time, including before trial. After the close of evidence, a party has the right to file requests for instructions on issues that could not reasonably have been anticipated at that earlier time. Additionally, the court may permit a party to file an untimely request. Rule 51(a).

rules.rule_statement: Before final arguments, the court must inform the parties of any instructions it proposes to give to the jury regarding its verdict. The court must also inform the parties of its proposed action on the parties’ requests for jury instructions. Rule 51(b).

rules.source_text:
Court’S Proposed Instructions
Before final arguments, the court must inform the parties of any instructions it proposes to give to the jury regarding its verdict. The court must also inform the parties of its proposed action on the parties’ requests for jury instructions. Rule 51(b).

rules.canonical_name: Party’S Objection To The Court’S Proposed Instructions
rules.rule_statement: The court must provide the parties with an opportunity to object to its proposed instructions on the record and out of the jury’s hearing before the instructions and arguments are delivered. Rule 51(b)(2). If the court fails to do this, a party’s objection is timely if it is made promptly after learning that an instruction has been or will be given or a request has been refused. Rule 51(c)(2). The party’s objection must be made on the record and specifically state the instruction or request objected to and the grounds for the objection. Rule 51(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: The court may instruct a jury regarding its verdict at any time before the jury is discharged. Consequently, the court may instruct the jury before as well as after the lawyers’ closing arguments. Rule 51(b)(3). Example: After beginning its deliberations, a jury sends the judge a note requesting additional instructions regarding the definition of a complex term of art. The judge is permitted to provide additional jury instructions regarding the term of art.

The judge is permitted to provide additional jury instructions regarding the term of art.

Juror’S Duties

rules.rule_statement: The court may excuse a juror for good cause not only before the submission of the case to the jury, but also during jury deliberations. Rule 47(c). Example: Sickness, family emergency, or juror misconduct can constitute good cause for excusing a juror. Example: Refusal of a juror to agree with other members of the jury does not constitute good cause for excusing a juror.

rules.rule_statement: There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each issue of ultimate fact. With a special verdict, the court submits written questions to the jury correlating to each ultimate fact of the case and asks the jury to make a finding on each fact (e.g., “Was the defendant negligent?”). The court may specifically direct the jury’s response to a question (e.g., “Yes or No”) or may permit the jury to respond with a brief answer (e.g., the percentage of a defendant’s fault in a negligence action). The judge then determines the legal consequences of those findings. Rule 49(a)(1). Even in a case based on diversity jurisdiction, the court is not bound by a state law that requires the use of a special verdict.
rules.short_rule_statement: There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each...
rules.source_text:
Form Of Verdict
There are three types of permissible verdicts. Rule 49. The type used is subject to the discretion of the court. A special verdict is a written finding made by the jury on each issue of ultimate fact. With a special verdict, the court submits written questions to the jury correlating to each ultimate fact of the case and asks the jury to make a finding on each fact (e.g., “Was the defendant negligent?”). The court may specifically direct the jury’s response to a question (e.g., “Yes or No”) or may permit the jury to respond with a brief answer (e.g., the percentage of a defendant’s fault in a negligence action). The judge then determines the legal consequences of those findings. Rule 49(a)(1). Even in a case based on diversity jurisdiction, the court is not bound by a state law that requires the use of a special verdict.

rules.rule_statement: A party waives the right to a jury trial on any issue of fact raised by the pleadings or evidence but not submitted to the jury, unless, before the jury retires, the party demands submission of the issue to the jury. Otherwise, the court may make a finding on that issue. When the court does not do so, it is deemed to have made a finding consistent with its judgment on the special verdict. Rule 49(a)(3). A general verdict is typically a decision by the jury regarding the prevailing party and, if the plaintiff is the prevailing party, the amount of damages. General verdicts are the usual form of a jury verdict in a civil case. A general verdict with special interrogatories couples a general verdict with a special verdict. It is used to ensure that the jury independently considered the material facts of the case in arriving at its verdict. If the answers to the interrogatories are consistent with each other but not with the general verdict, then the court may: general verdict, ii) Direct the jury to reconsider its answers and verdict, or iii) Order a new trial.

It is used to ensure that the jury independently considered the material facts of the case in arriving at its verdict. If the answers to the interrogatories are consistent with each other but not with the general verdict, then the court may:

rules.rule_statement: In cases of inconsistency between the answers, the court cannot enter a judgment, but must either direct the jury to reconsider its answers or order a new trial. Rule 49(b). Before the dismissal of the jury, if a party believes that the jury has returned an erroneous verdict that can be corrected, the party must raise this issue with the court. If the court believes that the jury did not properly follow its instructions, then the court may set aside the verdict and ask the jury to reconsider the issue. Alternatively, the court may order a new trial.

rules.rule_statement: After the jury returns a verdict and before the jury is dismissed, the court must poll the jurors individually if a party requests. The court may also do so on its own initiative. If there is a lack of unanimity or assent by the number of jurors to which the parties stipulated, the court may order the jury to continue deliberations, or it may order a new trial. Rule 48(c).

Polling
After the jury returns a verdict and before the jury is dismissed, the court must poll the jurors individually if a party requests. The court may also do so on its own initiative. If there is a lack of unanimity or assent by the number of jurors to which the parties stipulated, the court may order the jury to continue deliberations, or it may order a new trial. Rule 48(c).

To obtain a new trial, a party must not only demonstrate that the juror failed to answer honestly a material question on voir dire, but also show that a proper response would have provided a valid basis for a challenge for cause. The court may dismiss a juror or, if necessary, order a new trial if:

rules.canonical_name: The Juror Violates The Confidentiality Of Deliberations
rules.rule_statement: ii) The juror is improperly influenced by nonjurors; or iii) The juror investigates facts outside of those presented at trial. While generally a juror may not impeach one’s own verdict, a juror may testify about whether extraneous prejudicial information was improperly brought to the jury’s attention or whether any outside influence was improperly brought to bear on a juror. rules.rule_statement: A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a plaintiff may file a motion for prejudgment attachment as allowed by the state where the court is located. Federal statute governs the extent to which the state statute may be applied. Remedies include arrest, attachment, garnishment, replevin, sequestration, and other equivalent remedies. In addition to filing the motion, the plaintiff must give the defendant notice and an opportunity to be heard by preparing a writ of attachment in addition to a summons and complaint. A court will grant a prejudgment attachment only if the plaintiff shows that there is a likelihood of success of recovering an amount equal to the amount being sought to attach. Rule 64.
rules.short_rule_statement: A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a...
rules.source_text:
Prejudgment Attachment
A prejudgment attachment provides for seizing a person or property to secure satisfaction of a potential judgment. At the commencement and through the course of an action, a plaintiff may file a motion for prejudgment attachment as allowed by the state where the court is located. Federal statute governs the extent to which the state statute may be applied. Remedies include arrest, attachment, garnishment, replevin, sequestration, and other equivalent remedies. In addition to filing the motion, the plaintiff must give the defendant notice and an opportunity to be heard by preparing a writ of attachment in addition to a summons and complaint. A court will grant a prejudgment attachment only if the plaintiff shows that there is a likelihood of success of recovering an amount equal to the amount being sought to attach. Rule 64.

rules.canonical_name: Judgment As A Matter Of Law
rules.rule_statement: Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such challenges: motion for judgment as a matter of law and motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict. A motion for summary judgment as a matter of law is a motion that is made under Rule 50(a) before submission of the case to the jury. It is also called a motion for a directed verdict. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is made under Rule 50(b) by a party after the verdict and entry of judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such...
rules.source_text:
Judgment As A Matter Of Law
Rule 50 provides procedural requirements for challenging the sufficiency of the evidence in a civil jury trial. The Rule establishes two separate but related stages for such challenges: motion for judgment as a matter of law and motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict. A motion for summary judgment as a matter of law is a motion that is made under Rule 50(a) before submission of the case to the jury. It is also called a motion for a directed verdict. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. A motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict is made under Rule 50(b) by a party after the verdict and entry of judgment.
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law

rules.canonical_name: Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a party if the court finds that there is insufficient evidence for a jury reasonably to find for that party. The court may also grant such a motion against the party on any claim or defense that is dependent on a favorable finding on that issue.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a...
rules.source_text:
Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)
Under Rule 50(a), once a party has been fully heard on an issue at a jury trial, the court may grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law resolving the issue against a party if the court finds that there is insufficient evidence for a jury reasonably to find for that party. The court may also grant such a motion against the party on any claim or defense that is dependent on a favorable finding on that issue.
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law
Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (“Directed Verdict”)

rules.rule_statement: The court must view the evidence in the light most favorable to the opposing party and draw all reasonable inferences from the evidence in favor of the opposing party. The court may not consider the credibility of witnesses or evaluate the weight of the evidence, and it must disregard all evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe. If reasonable persons can draw different inferences, then the issue is for the jury to decide, and the motion cannot be granted.
rules.short_rule_statement: The court must view the evidence in the light most favorable to the opposing party and draw all reasonable inferences from the evidence in favor of the opposing party. The...
rules.source_text:
Standard
The court must view the evidence in the light most favorable to the opposing party and draw all reasonable inferences from the evidence in favor of the opposing party. The court may not consider the credibility of witnesses or evaluate the weight of the evidence, and it must disregard all evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe. If reasonable persons can draw different inferences, then the issue is for the jury to decide, and the motion cannot be granted.
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law

rules.rule_statement: A motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made at any time before the case is submitted to the jury. The motion must specify the judgment sought and the law and facts that entitle the movant to the judgment.
rules.short_rule_statement: A motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made at any time before the case is submitted to the jury. The motion must specify the judgment sought and the law and facts...
rules.source_text:
Procedure And Timing
A motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made at any time before the case is submitted to the jury. The motion must specify the judgment sought and the law and facts that entitle the movant to the judgment.
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law

rules.rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a defendant, instead of making a motion for a judgment as a matter of law, can move for a judgment on partial findings if the plaintiff failed to prove his case. Rule 52(c).
rules.short_rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a defendant, instead of making a motion for a judgment as a matter of law, can move for a judgment on partial findings if the plaintiff failed to prove his...
rules.source_text:
Judgment On Partial Findings
In a nonjury trial, a defendant, instead of making a motion for a judgment as a matter of law, can move for a judgment on partial findings if the plaintiff failed to prove his case. Rule 52(c).
topic_path: Judgment As A Matter Of Law

rules.canonical_name: Renewed Motion For Judgment As A Matter Of Law (Jnov)
rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 50(b), if the court does not grant a motion for judgment as a matter of law, then the court is considered to have submitted the action to the jury subject to the court’s later deciding the legal questions raised by the motion. The movant may file a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law no later than 28 days after the entry of judgment. If the motion addresses a jury issue not decided by a verdict, then the renewed motion must be filed no later than 28 days after the jury was discharged. A post-verdict motion under Rule 50(b) is only a renewal of the earlier motion made at the close of the evidence, and it can be granted only on grounds advanced in the pre-verdict motion. Therefore, filing a motion under Rule 50(a) for a judgment as a matter of law before the case is submitted to the jury is a requirement for consideration of a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law under Rule 50(b). If a party fails to move for a judgment as a matter of law before the case goes to the jury, then an appellate court will not review whether a district court erred in refusing to grant the party’s renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law. In ruling on the renewed motion, the court may:

Judgment
A judgment is a decree or order by a court that resolves the parties’ rights and demands for relief in a manner that permits it to be appealed Rule 54(a). See § IX.B. Appeals, infra.

rules.rule_statement: In general, a judgment should grant a party the relief to which the party is entitled, even if the party has not demanded such relief in its pleadings. However, a default judgment must not differ in kind or exceed in amount what is demanded in the pleadings. Rule 54(c).

rules.source_text:
Agreement With Pleadings
In general, a judgment should grant a party the relief to which the party is entitled, even if the party has not demanded such relief in its pleadings. However, a default judgment must not differ in kind or exceed in amount what is demanded in the pleadings. Rule 54(c).

rules.rule_statement: Unless a federal statute, rule, or court order provides otherwise, the prevailing party is allowed court costs, other than attorney’s fees, without needing to file a motion. The court clerk may tax costs on 14 days’ notice. A party may challenge the clerk’s action by serving a motion within seven days. Rule 54(d)(1). By serving on an opposing party an offer to allow judgment on specified terms at least 14 days before the date set for trial, a defending party may limit those costs to the costs then accrued if:

rules.source_text:
Costs
Unless a federal statute, rule, or court order provides otherwise, the prevailing party is allowed court costs, other than attorney’s fees, without needing to file a motion. The court clerk may tax costs on 14 days’ notice. A party may challenge the clerk’s action by serving a motion within seven days. Rule 54(d)(1). By serving on an opposing party an offer to allow judgment on specified terms at least 14 days before the date set for trial, a defending party may limit those costs to the costs then accrued if:

rules.canonical_name: The Opposing Party Does Not Accept The Offer;
rules.rule_statement: ii) The judgment is less favorable to the opposing party than the offer. A similar offer may be made once a party is determined to be liable, but damages have not been determined. Evidence of an unaccepted offer is not admissible except in a proceeding to determine costs. Rule 68.

rules.rule_statement: Unless a statute or court order provides otherwise, a claim for attorney’s fees that is not required by law to be proved at trial as an element of damages must be made by a motion filed within 14 days after entry of judgment. Rule 54(d)(2).

Attorney’S Fees
Unless a statute or court order provides otherwise, a claim for attorney’s fees that is not required by law to be proved at trial as an element of damages must be made by a motion filed within 14 days after entry of judgment. Rule 54(d)(2).

rules.canonical_name: Motion To Amend Or Make Additional Findings
rules.rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a party may make a motion for the court to amend its findings or to make additional findings. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of judgment and may be combined with a new trial motion. Rule 52(b). On appeal, a court’s finding of facts can be set aside only if clearly erroneous. The reviewing court must give consideration to the trial court’s opportunity to judge the witnesses’ credibility. Rule 52(a)(6).
rules.short_rule_statement: In a nonjury trial, a party may make a motion for the court to amend its findings or to make additional findings. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of...
rules.source_text:
Motion To Amend Or Make Additional Findings
In a nonjury trial, a party may make a motion for the court to amend its findings or to make additional findings. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of judgment and may be combined with a new trial motion. Rule 52(b). On appeal, a court’s finding of facts can be set aside only if clearly erroneous. The reviewing court must give consideration to the trial court’s opportunity to judge the witnesses’ credibility. Rule 52(a)(6).

rules.canonical_name: Motion To Alter Or Amend A Judgment
rules.rule_statement: A party may make a motion for the court to alter or amend a judgment. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of the judgment. Rule 59(e).
rules.short_rule_statement: A party may make a motion for the court to alter or amend a judgment. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of the judgment. Rule 59(e).

Motion To Alter Or Amend A Judgment
A party may make a motion for the court to alter or amend a judgment. This motion must be made within 28 days of the entry of the judgment. Rule 59(e).

rules.rule_statement: Under Rule 59(a), on motion by a party or on its own, the court may grant a new trial on all issues or concerning only certain issues or parties. Rule 59 does not specifically list the grounds that will justify a new trial. In practice, the overarching justification for a new trial is that the court may grant a new trial to prevent a miscarriage of justice. Some of the reasons that have been held to justify a new trial include:

Grounds
Under Rule 59(a), on motion by a party or on its own, the court may grant a new trial on all issues or concerning only certain issues or parties. Rule 59 does not specifically list the grounds that will justify a new trial. In practice, the overarching justification for a new trial is that the court may grant a new trial to prevent a miscarriage of justice. Some of the reasons that have been held to justify a new trial include:

rules.canonical_name: Error At Trial That Renders The Judgment Unfair
rules.rule_statement: ii) Newly discovered evidence that existed at the time of the trial was excusably overlooked and would likely have altered the outcome of the trial; iii) Prejudicial misconduct of counsel, a party, the judge, or a juror; iv) A verdict that is against the clear weight of the evidence;

rules.canonical_name: A Verdict That Is Based On False Evidence;
rules.rule_statement: vi) A verdict that is excessive or inadequate. In general, whether a new trial is warranted rests within the sound discretion of the trial court. Under Rule 61, the court must disregard all errors and defects that do not affect any party’s substantial rights. This is the “harmless error” rule. After giving the parties notice and an opportunity to be heard, the court may grant a timely motion for a new trial for a reason not stated in a party’s motion. The court must specify the reasons in its order.

rules.rule_statement: A motion for remittitur is a request to reduce a jury award of excessive damages. If the court determines that a verdict was excessive, then it may offer a reduction of the verdict, known as a remittitur, and grant a new trial on the condition that the remittitur is not accepted. If the court determines that the verdict was inadequate, then it only has the option of ordering a new trial. An additur (enhanced judgment) is not permitted.
rules.short_rule_statement: A motion for remittitur is a request to reduce a jury award of excessive damages. If the court determines that a verdict was excessive, then it may offer a reduction of the...
rules.source_text:
Remittitur
A motion for remittitur is a request to reduce a jury award of excessive damages. If the court determines that a verdict was excessive, then it may offer a reduction of the verdict, known as a remittitur, and grant a new trial on the condition that the remittitur is not accepted. If the court determines that the verdict was inadequate, then it only has the option of ordering a new trial. An additur (enhanced judgment) is not permitted.

rules.rule_statement: A motion for a new trial must be filed no later than 28 days after the entry of judgment. Rule 59(b). When the motion is based on affidavits, those affidavits must be filed with the motion. The opposing party then has 14 days after being served to file opposing affidavits. The court may permit reply affidavits. Rule 59(c). Montgomery Ward & Co. v. Duncan, 311 U.S. 243 (1940). See Hetzel v. Prince William County., 523 U.S. 208 (1998).
